location: 3 cun above the transverse crease of the wrist, between the tendons of palmaris longus and flexor carpi radialis
indications: Contracture of the elbow and arm
boardPearl: Jing-River/Metal point of the Pericardium channel. Classically important for malaria (alternating chills and fever) — the intermediary that mediates between hot and cold. Treats mental disorders and epilepsy. Metal on a Fire channel — helps control excess Fire.
memoryTrick: Intermediary Courier = a messenger that goes between (intermediary). Located between PC-4 and PC-6 (the two most famous PC forearm points). The courier mediates between febrile and cold states (malaria).

name: Nei Guan
location: 2 cun above the transverse crease of the wrist, between the tendons of palmaris longus and flexor carpi radialis
categories: Confluent point of Yin Wei Mai
indications: Contracture and pain of the elbow and arm
boardPearl: One of the most important points in acupuncture. Luo-Connecting point — connects PC to SJ. Confluent point of the Yin Wei Mai — paired with SP-4 (Chong Mai). Together PC-6/SP-4 treat the Heart, chest, and Stomach. THE point for nausea, vomiting, and morning sickness (evidence-based). Opens the chest, calms the Heart, harmonizes the Stomach. Unbinds the chest for chest Bi syndrome.
memoryTrick: Inner Pass = the inner gate/pass to the Heart. Located on the inner (palmar) forearm, 2 cun above the wrist. The pass opens inward to the Heart and Stomach. Paired with SJ-5 Wai Guan (Outer Pass) on the opposite side of the arm. Remember: Inner = Yin = Heart/nausea; Outer = Yang = fever/exterior.

name: Da Ling
location: At the midpoint of the transverse crease of the wrist, between the tendons of palmaris longus and flexor carpi radialis
boardPearl: Shu-Stream/Earth point and Yuan-Source point — the primary point for accessing the Pericardium's Qi directly. Also one of Sun Si Miao's 13 Ghost points (Gui Xin — Ghost Heart) for treating mental illness, mania, and possession. Earth on a Fire channel — Earth controls and grounds the Fire. Calms the Shen powerfully.
memoryTrick: Great Mound = a great mound (Earth) at the wrist crease. The great mound is the Yuan-Source — the great origin of PC Qi. Also a Ghost point — the ghost hides in the great mound. Located right at the wrist crease — easy to find between the two tendons.
cautions: Located near the median nerve — needle carefully

name: Lao Gong
location: In the center of the palm, between the second and third metacarpal bones, proximal to the metacarpophalangeal joint. When the fist is clenched, the point is where the tip of the middle finger touches the palm
boardPearl: Ying-Spring/Fire point and Tonification point. Fire within Fire — the Horary point, most powerfully tonifies the Pericardium. Also a Ghost point (Gui Lu — Ghost Road). Clears Heart Fire — treats mouth sores, mental disorders, and mania. Fire is the mother of Earth but here the key point is that Fire on Fire = maximum Heart/PC tonification. The palm is hot = Fire.
memoryTrick: Palace of Toil = the palm that toils (works). The palace (center of the palm) is where you feel heat when the Heart is agitated. Fire on Fire (Ying-Spring/Fire on PC/Fire) = the hottest point. Also a Ghost point — ghosts toil in the palace. Found where the middle finger tip touches the palm when fist is clenched.
cautions: May be painful — the palm is sensitive

name: Zhong Chong
location: At the center of the tip of the middle finger
depth: 0.1 cun perpendicularly, or prick to bleed
indications: Stiffness and swelling of the tongue
boardPearl: Jing-Well/Wood point of the Pericardium channel. Like all Jing-Well points, used for emergency revival and loss of consciousness. Pricked to bleed for clearing Heat and restoring consciousness. Wood is the mother of Fire but does not serve as the tonification point (PC-8 is the tonification point). Central Hub reflects the middle finger = center of the hand.
memoryTrick: Central Hub = the central (middle) finger is the hub of the PC channel. The Qi rushes (Chong) through the center. Prick the tip to revive consciousness — the most distal and acute emergency point on the PC channel.
cautions: Prick to bleed for emergencies; painful to needle

name: Guan Chong
location: On the ring finger, 0.1 cun proximal to the ulnar corner of the nail
depth: 0.1 cun superficially, or prick to bleed
boardPearl: Jing-Well/Metal point of the San Jiao channel. Like all Well points, used for emergency revival and clearing Heat. Metal on a Fire (Yang) channel — following the Yang Five Shu element sequence. Pricked to bleed for sore throat, febrile diseases, and acute Heat conditions.
memoryTrick: Passage Hub = the passage (Guan) where Qi begins to rush (Chong) on the SJ channel. At the ring finger's ulnar nail corner. Well points are always at the fingertips — the hub where channel Qi begins.

name: Ye Men
location: On the dorsum of the hand, proximal to the margin of the web between the ring and little fingers, at the junction of the red and white skin
indications: Pain in the hand and arm
boardPearl: Ying-Spring/Water point of the San Jiao channel. Water on a Fire channel — helps clear Heat. The name 'Fluid Gate' reflects the SJ's role in regulating fluid metabolism. Treats ear disorders (deafness, tinnitus) — the SJ channel encircles the ear. Clears Heat from the Shaoyang level.
memoryTrick: Fluid Gate = the gate of fluids, reflecting the San Jiao's role in water metabolism. The Water point on the SJ — the organ that regulates the water passages. Located between the ring and little finger web.

name: Zhong Zhu
location: On the dorsum of the hand, in the depression proximal to the 4th and 5th metacarpophalangeal joints
indications: Pain in the elbow and arm
boardPearl: Shu-Stream/Wood point and Tonification point of the San Jiao channel. Wood is the mother of Fire — tonifies via the mother-child rule. Important for ear disorders (deafness, tinnitus) — one of the most used distal points for ear problems. Also treats temporal headache (Shaoyang headache).
memoryTrick: Central Islet = an islet (small island) in the center of the dorsal hand. Not to be confused with KD-15 Zhong Zhu (Central Flow — different characters). As the tonification point, this islet is a source of strength for the SJ channel. Think: island of power for the ears.

name: Yang Chi
location: On the dorsum of the wrist, in the depression ulnar to the tendon of extensor digitorum communis, at the level of the dorsal wrist crease
indications: Pain in the shoulder and arm
boardPearl: Yuan-Source point of the San Jiao channel. 'Yang Pool' = the pool of Yang Qi. As Yuan-Source, it accesses the original Qi of the San Jiao — the organ responsible for the transformation and transportation of Yuan Qi throughout the body. Important for diabetes (Xiao Ke/wasting-thirsting) reflecting the SJ's role in fluid metabolism.
memoryTrick: Yang Pool = a pool of Yang on the dorsal (Yang) wrist. The Yuan-Source point pools original Yang Qi. The San Jiao distributes Yuan Qi — this is its source point. Think: the pool from which all Yang warmth originates.

name: Wai Guan
location: 2 cun above the dorsal wrist crease, between the radius and ulna
categories: Confluent point of Yang Wei Mai
indications: Pain and motor impairment of the elbow and arm
boardPearl: Luo-Connecting point — connects SJ to PC. Confluent point of the Yang Wei Mai — paired with GB-41 (Dai Mai). Together SJ-5/GB-41 treat the sides of the body, ears, and outer canthus. Major point for releasing the exterior — especially Wind-Heat and Shaoyang level disorders. Directly opposite PC-6 on the forearm. One of the most important points for treating exterior pathogenic factors.
memoryTrick: Outer Pass = the outer gate/pass to the exterior. Directly opposite PC-6 (Inner Pass) on the other side of the arm. Inner Pass = Yin/interior; Outer Pass = Yang/exterior. The pass opens outward to expel pathogens. Remember: SJ-5 + GB-41 = Yang Wei Mai — the Yang Linking Vessel links all Yang channels.

name: Zhi Gou
location: 3 cun above the dorsal wrist crease, between the radius and ulna, on the radial side of the extensor digitorum communis
indications: Pain in the chest and hypochondrium
Pain in the shoulder and back and arm
boardPearl: Jing-River/Fire point of the San Jiao channel — the Horary point (Fire on Fire). One of the single most important distal points for constipation, especially from Heat or Qi stagnation. Treats lateral costal pain (Shaoyang region). Regulates Qi in the San Jiao to move the bowels. Also important for sudden loss of voice and hypochondriac pain.
memoryTrick: Branch Ditch = a branching ditch/channel that moves things along. Think of a ditch that drains — it moves stagnation. The #1 point association: SJ-6 = constipation. The ditch unblocks the passage. Also: Fire on Fire = Horary point.

name: Hui Zong
location: 3 cun above the dorsal wrist crease, on the radial side of the ulna, one finger-breadth ulnar to SJ-6
boardPearl: Xi-Cleft point of the San Jiao channel. Xi-Cleft points treat acute conditions and pain. For Yang channels, Xi-Cleft points treat acute pain (not bleeding as in Yin channels). At the same level as SJ-6 but one finger-breadth ulnar. Important for acute ear conditions (sudden deafness) and acute arm pain.
memoryTrick: Convergence and Gathering = Qi converges and gathers at the Xi-Cleft. Located at the same height as SJ-6 but offset to the ulnar side — they are neighbors. Think: Qi gathers deeply here for acute SJ emergencies.

name: San Yang Luo
location: 4 cun above the dorsal wrist crease, between the radius and ulna
indications: Pain in the hand and arm
boardPearl: The name 'Three Yang Connection' reflects the point's role in connecting all three Yang channels of the arm (LI, SI, SJ). Treats sudden loss of voice (aphonia) and deafness. The three Yang channels converge here through their Luo connections.
memoryTrick: Three Yang Connection = where the three arm Yang channels (LI, SI, SJ) connect. Think: three friends meeting — the three Yang channels of the hand all link up at this point.

name: Si Du
location: 5 cun below the olecranon, between the radius and ulna
boardPearl: Located on the posterior forearm, 5 cun below the olecranon. 'Four Rivers' = the four rivers of Qi flowing through the SJ channel. Treats similar conditions to SJ-8: sudden loss of voice, deafness. Not frequently tested individually.
memoryTrick: Four Rivers = four rivers of Qi flow through this area. Think of four streams running down the forearm. Located 5 cun below the olecranon — midway along the forearm.

name: Tian Jing
location: In the depression 1 cun superior to the olecranon, when the elbow is flexed
indications: Pain in the neck and shoulder and arm and elbow
boardPearl: He-Sea/Earth point and Sedation point of the San Jiao channel. Earth is the child of Fire — sedates via the mother-child rule. Major point for scrofula (lymph node swellings), goiter, and phlegm nodules. Transforms Phlegm and dissipates nodules. Important for lateral elbow pain and neck/shoulder disorders. Treats depression and emotional disorders.
memoryTrick: Celestial Well = a heavenly well (He-Sea) above the olecranon. The well is dug in the earth (Earth point) at the elbow. Think: the Sedation point drains excess Fire down a celestial well. Key association: phlegm nodules, scrofula, goiter — lumps near the neck.

name: Qing Leng Yuan
location: 1 cun above SJ-10, on the posterior aspect of the arm
indications: Pain in the shoulder and arm
boardPearl: Located 1 cun above SJ-10 (2 cun above the olecranon). 'Clear Cold Abyss' = a cool, deep place that clears Heat. Rarely tested as an individual point. Mainly treats local arm and shoulder pain.
memoryTrick: Clear Cold Abyss = a clear, cold, deep abyss above the elbow. The cold abyss cools down heat and inflammation. Located 1 cun above the Celestial Well (SJ-10).

name: Xiao Luo
location: On the posterior aspect of the upper arm, on the line connecting SJ-10 and SJ-13, 5 cun above the olecranon
boardPearl: Located midway on the posterior upper arm. 'Dispersing Luo' suggests dispersing Qi through the Luo collaterals. Treats headache, neck stiffness, and local arm pain. Seldom tested individually.
memoryTrick: Dispersing Luo River = Qi disperses through the Luo network like a river spreading into streams. Located on the back of the upper arm, midway up.

name: Nao Hui
location: On the posterior aspect of the upper arm, on the line connecting SJ-10 and SJ-14, at the posterior border of the deltoid muscle, about 3 cun below SJ-14
indications: Pain in the shoulder and arm
boardPearl: Located on the posterior upper arm at the deltoid border. 'Upper Arm Meeting' = where channels meet on the upper arm. Treats goiter and scrofula like SJ-10. The SJ channel is important for treating phlegm nodules in the neck/thyroid region.
memoryTrick: Upper Arm Meeting = the meeting point on the upper arm (Nao = upper arm). The channels gather here before ascending to the shoulder.

name: Jian Liao
location: On the shoulder, posterior and inferior to the lateral end of the acromion, in the depression about 1 cun posterior to LI-15, when the arm is abducted
indications: Pain and motor impairment of the shoulder
boardPearl: Important local point for shoulder disorders, particularly posterior shoulder pain. Located posterior to LI-15 — while LI-15 is at the anterolateral acromion, SJ-14 is at the posterolateral aspect. Together with LI-15 and SI-9/10, forms a key group for shoulder pain treatment.
memoryTrick: Shoulder Crevice = in the crevice (depression) of the shoulder behind the acromion. Think of the bony crevice you find when the arm is abducted. SJ-14 is posterior to LI-15 — SJ channel runs behind LI channel on the shoulder.

name: Tian Liao
location: Midway between GB-21 and SI-13, on the superior angle of the scapula
indications: Pain and stiffness of the neck and shoulder
boardPearl: Located at the superior angle of the scapula, midway between GB-21 and SI-13. 'Celestial Crevice' = a heavenly crevice at the top of the shoulder. Treats neck, shoulder, and upper back pain. The three channels (SJ, GB, SI) converge in the shoulder/scapular region.
memoryTrick: Celestial Crevice = a heavenly crevice at the top of the scapula. Located between GB-21 (top of the shoulder) and SI-13 (scapula). Think: celestial = high up on the body, crevice = in the bony depression.
cautions: Do not puncture deeply — risk of pneumothorax

name: Tian You
location: On the lateral side of the neck, posterior and inferior to the mastoid process, at the posterior border of the sternocleidomastoid muscle, level with the angle of the mandible
indications: Swelling and pain of the face
boardPearl: Window of Heaven point. Windows of Heaven regulate the ascending and descending of Qi between the head and body, treating disorders of the head, face, and sense organs. SJ-16 specifically treats sudden deafness and facial swelling. Know all Window of Heaven points for the exam.
memoryTrick: Celestial Window = Window of Heaven on the SJ channel. Located on the neck behind the SCM. The window opens to heaven (the head), allowing Qi to flow to the sense organs — especially the ears (SJ channel circles the ear).

name: Yi Feng
location: Behind the earlobe, in the depression between the mandible and the mastoid process
boardPearl: One of the most important SJ points. Located directly behind the earlobe. THE primary point for ear disorders (tinnitus, deafness) and facial paralysis (Bell's palsy). 'Wind Screen' = shields against Wind invading the ear and face. Commonly combined with ST-6, ST-7, LI-4 for facial paralysis. Also treats mumps and parotid gland disorders.
memoryTrick: Wind Screen = a screen that blocks Wind from attacking the ear and face. Located right behind the ear — like a screen protecting it. Key associations: tinnitus + deafness + facial paralysis + mumps. If the question is about the ear or Bell's palsy, think SJ-17.

name: Qi Mai
location: On the mastoid process, at the junction of the middle and lower third of the curve formed by SJ-17 and SJ-20, behind the helix of the ear
depth: 0.1-0.3 cun subcutaneously, or prick to bleed
boardPearl: Located on the mastoid process behind the ear. 'Spasm Vessel' reflects its use for infantile convulsions and spasms. Part of the group of peri-auricular SJ points (SJ-17 to SJ-21) that treat ear disorders. Often pricked to bleed for acute conditions.
memoryTrick: Spasm Vessel = treats spasms and convulsions, especially in children. Located on the bony mastoid behind the ear, between SJ-17 and SJ-20 along the curve of the ear.
cautions: Avoid the superficial temporal artery

name: Lu Xi
location: On the mastoid process, at the junction of the upper and middle third of the curve formed by SJ-17 and SJ-20, behind the helix of the ear
boardPearl: Located on the mastoid above SJ-18, behind the ear. 'Skull Rest' = resting on the skull (mastoid bone). Treats similar conditions to other peri-auricular SJ points: ear disorders, headache, convulsions. Not commonly tested individually.
memoryTrick: Skull Rest = resting on the skull bone (mastoid). Between SJ-18 and SJ-20, ascending behind the ear. The skull rests here — a quiet place behind the ear.

name: Jiao Sun
location: Directly above the ear apex, within the hairline
indications: Redness and swelling of the ear
boardPearl: Located directly above the ear apex within the hairline. 'Minute Angle' = the fine angle above the ear. Treats ear, eye, and dental disorders. The SJ channel winds around the ear — SJ-20 is the highest point in the peri-auricular curve before the channel turns toward the eyebrow.
memoryTrick: Minute Angle = the small (minute) angle at the top of the ear. Think of the angle the channel makes as it curves over the top of the ear. Above the apex of the ear, within the hairline.

name: Er Men
location: In the depression anterior to the supratragic notch, at the posterior border of the condyloid process of the mandible, when the mouth is open
indications: Stiffness and pain of the lip and jaw
boardPearl: One of the three peri-auricular points used for ear disorders: SI-19 (Ting Gong), SJ-21 (Er Men), GB-2 (Ting Hui) — all located anterior to the ear. SJ-21 is the most superior of the three. 'Ear Gate' = the gate to the ear. The three points are needled with the mouth open. Board favorite: know the order SI-19, SJ-21, GB-2 from middle to top to bottom (or remember all three are anterior to the tragus).
memoryTrick: Ear Gate = the gate of the ear. The SJ channel encircles the ear, and this is the main gate. Located anterior to the ear with mouth open. Remember the ear trio: SI-19 (Ting Gong/Auditory Palace), SJ-21 (Er Men/Ear Gate), GB-2 (Ting Hui/Auditory Convergence).
cautions: Needle with mouth open; avoid the superficial temporal artery

name: Er He Liao
location: Anterior and superior to SJ-21, on the posterior border of the hairline of the temple, level with the root of the ear, posterior to the superficial temporal artery
boardPearl: Located anterior-superior to SJ-21, at the temple near the ear root. 'Ear Harmony Crevice' = creating harmony for the ear. Treats tinnitus, trismus, and lateral headache. The SJ channel passes through the temple region here before reaching SJ-23 at the eyebrow.
memoryTrick: Ear Harmony Crevice = harmonizing the ear from its crevice at the temple. Located just in front of and above the ear — the channel is heading toward the eyebrow (SJ-23). Nearly at the end of the SJ journey.
cautions: Avoid the superficial temporal artery

name: Si Zhu Kong
location: In the depression at the lateral end of the eyebrow
depth: 0.3-0.5 cun subcutaneously along the eyebrow, or prick to bleed
boardPearl: Final point of the San Jiao channel, at the lateral end of the eyebrow. The channel then connects internally to GB-1 (at the outer canthus) to begin the Gallbladder channel. Important for eye disorders, facial paralysis, and lateral headache. 'Silk Bamboo' refers to the eyebrow hairs resembling silk bamboo leaves. Moxibustion is classically contraindicated here.
memoryTrick: Silk Bamboo Hollow = the hollow at the end of the silk bamboo (eyebrow). The eyebrow hairs look like delicate silk bamboo leaves, and the point is in the hollow (depression) at their lateral end. Last stop on the SJ before connecting to GB-1.
cautions: Moxibustion is contraindicated according to classical texts

name: Tong Zi Liao
location: On the face, in the depression lateral to the outer canthus of the eye, on the lateral side of the orbit
depth: 0.3-0.5 cun subcutaneously posteriorly along the orbital margin
categories: Meeting point of GB, SI, and SJ
Entry point of the Gallbladder channel
indications: Deviation of the eye and mouth
Redness and pain of the eye
boardPearl: Meeting point of GB, SI, and SJ. Entry point of the Gallbladder channel. GB-1 is the starting point of the Gallbladder channel at the outer canthus. Important for all eye disorders, especially lateral eye issues. The Gallbladder channel begins and ends at the head/foot.
memoryTrick: Pupil Bone-Hole = a hole near the pupil at the bone of the orbit. The pupil (Tong Zi) looks out from this bone hole (Liao). Three Yang channels of the hand (GB, SI, SJ) meet at the eye.
cautions: Avoid puncturing the eyeball; needle subcutaneously along the orbital margin
specialCategories: Meeting point of GB, SI, SJ

name: Ting Hui
location: On the face, anterior to the intertragic notch, at the posterior border of the condyloid process of the mandible, in the depression formed when the mouth is open
depth: 0.5-1 cun perpendicularly with the mouth open
categories: Meeting point of GB and SJ
indications: Deviation of the eye and mouth
boardPearl: Meeting point of GB and SJ. Located anterior to the ear, this point is crucial for hearing disorders. Compare with SI-19 (anterior to tragus) and SJ-21 (above SI-19). All three ear points are needled with the mouth open.
memoryTrick: Auditory Convergence = hearing (auditory) converges here in front of the ear. The Gallbladder channel passes around the ear, and GB-2 is the main GB ear point.
cautions: Needle with mouth open to locate the depression; avoid the superficial temporal artery

name: Shang Guan
location: In front of the ear, on the upper border of the zygomatic arch, in the depression directly above ST-7
categories: Meeting point of GB, SJ, and ST
indications: Deviation of the eye and mouth
boardPearl: Meeting point of GB, SJ, and ST. Located on the upper border of the zygomatic arch directly above ST-7 (Xiaguan/Lower Gate). The Upper Gate and Lower Gate frame the TMJ region.
memoryTrick: Upper Gate = the upper gate of the jaw (above the zygomatic arch). ST-7 Xiaguan is the Lower Gate. Together they flank the jaw joint.
cautions: Do not needle deeply; avoid the superficial temporal artery. Do not needle with mouth open.
specialCategories: Meeting point of GB, SJ, ST

name: Han Yan
location: Within the hairline of the temporal region, at the junction of the upper quarter and lower three quarters of the distance between ST-8 and GB-7
categories: Meeting point of GB, ST, and SJ
Meeting point of GB and Yang Wei Mai
boardPearl: Meeting point of GB, ST, SJ, and Yang Wei Mai. Part of the temporal cluster of GB points (GB-4 through GB-7). These points are all needled subcutaneously along the temporal region.
memoryTrick: Forehead Fullness = a feeling of distension/fullness in the forehead. Used for temporal headaches and migraines.
specialCategories: Meeting point of GB, ST, SJ, Yang Wei Mai

name: Xuan Lu
location: Within the hairline of the temporal region, at the midpoint of the distance between ST-8 and GB-7
categories: Meeting point of GB, ST, LI, and SJ
Meeting point of GB and Yang Wei Mai
indications: Pain and swelling of the outer canthus
boardPearl: Meeting point of GB, ST, LI, SJ, and Yang Wei Mai. One of the temporal cluster points — all treat migraine and lateral headache. The name 'Suspended Skull' highlights its location on the side of the cranium.
memoryTrick: Suspended Skull = hanging from the skull on the temporal region. Think of the temporal bone 'suspended' on the side of the head.
specialCategories: Meeting point of GB, ST, LI, SJ, Yang Wei Mai

name: Xuan Li
location: Within the hairline of the temporal region, at the junction of the lower quarter and upper three quarters of the distance between ST-8 and GB-7
categories: Meeting point of GB, ST, LI, and SJ
Meeting point of GB and Yang Wei Mai
boardPearl: Meeting point of GB, ST, LI, SJ, and Yang Wei Mai. The third point in the temporal cluster. 'Suspended Tuft' refers to the tuft of hair at the temple.
memoryTrick: Suspended Tuft = a tuft of hair hangs (is suspended) at the temple. Part of the GB temporal cluster for lateral headaches.
specialCategories: Meeting point of GB, ST, LI, SJ, Yang Wei Mai

name: Qu Bin
location: Within the hairline of the temporal region, anterior and superior to the auricular apex, approximately one fingerbreadth anterior to SJ-20
categories: Meeting point of GB and UB
indications: Swelling and pain of the cheek
Pain and stiffness of the neck
boardPearl: Meeting point of GB and UB. The last in the temporal cluster of GB points. Located anterior to the ear at the crook of the temple.
memoryTrick: Crook of the Temple = the crook (curve) at the temple, near where the sideburn curves around the ear.

name: Shuai Gu
location: Superior to the auricular apex, 1.5 cun within the hairline
categories: Meeting point of GB and UB
boardPearl: Meeting point of GB and UB. The classic 'hangover point' — treats headache and vomiting from excessive alcohol. This point is directly above the ear apex. The Gallbladder channel's close relationship with Liver means GB-8 addresses Liver-Gallbladder damp-heat rising to the head.
memoryTrick: Leading Valley = this valley leads (conducts) away the headache, especially from alcohol. Think 'hung over in the valley' — Shuai Gu for the morning after.

name: Tian Chong
location: Posterior to the auricular root, 2 cun within the hairline, 0.5 cun posterior to GB-8
categories: Meeting point of GB and UB
indications: Swelling and pain of the gums
boardPearl: Meeting point of GB and UB. Located behind the ear and above, rarely a primary exam point. Treats headache and gum disorders.
memoryTrick: Celestial Hub = a hub point in the heavens (head). Located posterior to GB-8, continuing the arc around the ear.

name: Fu Bai
location: Posterior and superior to the mastoid process, at the junction of the middle third and upper third of the curved line connecting GB-9 and GB-12
categories: Meeting point of GB and UB
indications: Pain and stiffness of the neck
boardPearl: Meeting point of GB and UB. Part of the posterior auricular cluster. Treats ear and neck disorders. Rarely featured prominently on board exams.
memoryTrick: Floating White = whiteness (pallor) floating on the surface. The point floats above the mastoid on the skull.

name: Tou Qiao Yin
location: Posterior and superior to the mastoid process, at the junction of the lower third and middle third of the curved line connecting GB-9 and GB-12
categories: Meeting point of GB and UB
boardPearl: Meeting point of GB and UB. The name 'Head Portal Yin' distinguishes this from GB-44 (Foot Qiao Yin). Portal to Yin at the head — the Gallbladder channel as Shaoyang is the hinge between Yin and Yang.
memoryTrick: Head Portal Yin = the portal (opening) to Yin at the head, as opposed to GB-44 Zu Qiao Yin (Foot Portal Yin). Head vs. Foot distinguishes the two Qiao Yin points.

name: Wan Gu
location: In the depression posteroinferior to the mastoid process
categories: Meeting point of GB and UB
indications: Pain and stiffness of the neck
boardPearl: Meeting point of GB and UB. Located behind the ear at the mastoid process. Important local point for headache, insomnia, and facial paralysis. Do not confuse with SI-4 Wan Gu (Wrist Bone) — same pinyin name, different character and location.
memoryTrick: Completion Bone = the bone (mastoid) where the skull is complete at the back. Two Wan Gu points exist: GB-12 (mastoid) and SI-4 (wrist). GB-12 is behind the ear.

name: Ben Shen
location: On the forehead, 0.5 cun within the hairline, at the junction of the medial two thirds and lateral one third of the distance from GV-24 to ST-8, 3 cun lateral to GV-24
categories: Meeting point of GB and Yang Wei Mai
boardPearl: Meeting point of GB and Yang Wei Mai. One of the most important GB points for spirit (Shen) disorders. The name 'Root of the Spirit' reveals its function — it roots and stabilizes the Shen. Key point for anxiety, insomnia, and epilepsy. The Gallbladder influences decision-making and courage, and this point addresses fear and indecision.
memoryTrick: Root of the Spirit = this point roots the spirit/mind. When the Shen (spirit) is uprooted (anxiety, mania, insomnia), GB-13 brings it back to its root. Think: 'Ben = root, Shen = spirit.'
specialCategories: Meeting point of GB, Yang Wei Mai

name: Yang Bai
location: On the forehead, 1 cun above the midpoint of the eyebrow, directly above the pupil when looking straight ahead
categories: Meeting point of GB, ST, LI, SJ, and Yang Wei Mai
boardPearl: Meeting point of GB, ST, LI, SJ, and Yang Wei Mai — five channels meet here. Key local point for frontal headache and facial paralysis. Located directly above the pupil, 1 cun above the midpoint of the eyebrow. Frequently combined with Tai Yang (extra point) and ST-8 for frontal headache.
memoryTrick: Yang White = bright (white/bai) Yang shining on the forehead like a headlight above the eye. Located 1 cun above the midpoint of the eyebrow, directly above the pupil.
specialCategories: Meeting point of GB, ST, LI, SJ, Yang Wei Mai

name: Tou Lin Qi
location: On the head, directly above the pupil, 0.5 cun within the hairline, midway between GV-24 and ST-8
categories: Meeting point of GB, UB, and Yang Wei Mai
boardPearl: Meeting point of GB, UB, and Yang Wei Mai. Do not confuse with GB-41 Zu Lin Qi (Foot Governor of Tears). Head Lin Qi (GB-15) is on the head; Foot Lin Qi (GB-41) is on the foot. Both relate to tears and eye disorders.
memoryTrick: Head Governor of Tears = governs lacrimation at the head. The 'head' prefix (Tou) distinguishes it from GB-41 Zu Lin Qi (Foot). Lin Qi = 'about to weep.'
specialCategories: Meeting point of GB, UB, Yang Wei Mai

name: Mu Chuang
location: On the head, 1.5 cun posterior to GB-15, 2.25 cun lateral to GV-21
categories: Meeting point of GB and Yang Wei Mai
boardPearl: Meeting point of GB and Yang Wei Mai. The name 'Eye Window' indicates its function of brightening the eyes. Located on the lateral aspect of the head between GB-15 and GB-17.
memoryTrick: Eye Window = a window for the eyes on the top of the head. The window opens to let light in and improve vision.
specialCategories: Meeting point of GB, Yang Wei Mai

name: Zheng Ying
location: On the head, 1.5 cun posterior to GB-16, 2.25 cun lateral to GV-20
categories: Meeting point of GB and Yang Wei Mai
boardPearl: Meeting point of GB and Yang Wei Mai. Part of the lateral head sequence of GB points. Treats headache and dental pain.
memoryTrick: Upright Construction = building up the Yang Qi at the vertex. Part of the Yang Wei Mai connection on the skull.
specialCategories: Meeting point of GB, Yang Wei Mai

name: Cheng Ling
location: On the head, 1.5 cun posterior to GB-17, 2.25 cun lateral to GV-19
categories: Meeting point of GB and Yang Wei Mai
boardPearl: Meeting point of GB and Yang Wei Mai. 'Receiving Spirit' indicates its role in calming the mind. Located on the parietal region of the skull.
memoryTrick: Receiving Spirit = this point receives and houses the spirit on the upper head. The GB channel on the skull is closely related to mental clarity.
specialCategories: Meeting point of GB, Yang Wei Mai

name: Nao Kong
location: On the head, directly above GB-20, level with GV-17, on the lateral side of the external occipital protuberance
categories: Meeting point of GB and Yang Wei Mai
boardPearl: Meeting point of GB and Yang Wei Mai. Located directly above GB-20 at the level of the external occipital protuberance. The name 'Brain Hollow' references its proximity to the brain cavity.
memoryTrick: Brain Hollow = a hollow (depression) near the brain at the occiput. Directly above the major point GB-20.
specialCategories: Meeting point of GB, Yang Wei Mai

name: Feng Chi
location: Below the occipital bone, in the depression between the upper portion of the sternocleidomastoid muscle and the upper portion of the trapezius muscle, level with GV-16
depth: 0.8-1.2 cun toward the tip of the contralateral eye (or the nose)
categories: Meeting point of GB, SJ, Yang Wei Mai, and Yang Qiao Mai
indications: Deviation of the eye and mouth
Pain and weakness of the lumbar spine
boardPearl: One of the most important and most commonly tested points. Meeting point of GB, SJ, Yang Wei Mai, and Yang Qiao Mai. MAJOR point for expelling all types of Wind (external and internal). Treats virtually all head/eye/ear/nose conditions. Key point for Wind-Cold, Wind-Heat, Liver Yang rising, Liver Wind, occipital headache, neck stiffness, and eye disorders. The name 'Wind Pool' indicates it is where Wind collects — needling it disperses Wind from the pool. Often paired with GV-16, GV-20, LI-4, and UB-12.
memoryTrick: Wind Pool = Wind collects in this pool at the back of the neck. To drain Wind from the head and neck, drain the pool. The most important point for Wind on the entire body — external Wind (colds), internal Wind (tremors, dizziness), and Liver Wind (stroke).
cautions: Direct needle toward contralateral eye or tip of nose. NEVER direct superiorly toward the brain — risk of puncturing the medulla oblongata. Deep needling is dangerous.
specialCategories: Meeting point of GB, SJ, Yang Wei Mai, Yang Qiao Mai

name: Jian Jing
location: On the shoulder, at the midpoint between the spinous process of C7 (GV-14) and the lateral end of the acromion, at the highest point of the shoulder
categories: Meeting point of GB, SJ, ST, and Yang Wei Mai
indications: Pain and stiffness of the neck
Pain and motor impairment of the shoulder and back
boardPearl: Meeting point of GB, SJ, ST, and Yang Wei Mai. CONTRAINDICATED IN PREGNANCY — this point has a strong downward-bearing action and can promote labor and expel the fetus. Used for difficult labor and to descend the placenta. The name 'Shoulder Well' indicates a deep well at the shoulder that can draw things downward. Strong needling sensation. Classic point for neck and shoulder tension.
memoryTrick: Shoulder Well = a deep well at the shoulder that pulls things downward. This descending action is why it promotes labor — and why it is FORBIDDEN in pregnancy. Think: 'the well pulls the baby down.'
cautions: CONTRAINDICATED IN PREGNANCY — strong descending action can induce labor or miscarriage. Do not needle deeply — risk of pneumothorax due to proximity to the lung apex.
specialCategories: Meeting point of GB, SJ, ST, Yang Wei Mai

name: Yuan Ye
location: On the lateral side of the chest, on the midaxillary line, 3 cun below the axilla, in the fifth intercostal space
indications: Pain and swelling of the axilla
boardPearl: Located on the mid-axillary line, 3 cun below the axilla. Treats lateral chest and axillary pain. Not a commonly tested point.
memoryTrick: Armpit Abyss = an abyss (deep space) below the armpit. Think of the hollow below the armpit.
cautions: Do not puncture deeply — risk of pneumothorax

name: Zhe Jin
location: On the lateral side of the chest, 1 cun anterior to GB-22, approximately level with the nipple, in the fourth intercostal space
boardPearl: Located anterior to GB-22 on the lateral chest wall. The name 'Sinew Seat' relates to the GB's role as the Influential point channel for sinews. Local point for lateral chest and rib pain.
memoryTrick: Sinew Seat = where the sinews of the lateral chest are seated. The GB channel governs sinews.
cautions: Do not puncture deeply — risk of pneumothorax

name: Ri Yue
location: On the chest, directly below the nipple, in the seventh intercostal space, one rib below LV-14
categories: Meeting point of GB, SP, and Yang Wei Mai
boardPearl: Front-Mu point of the Gallbladder. Meeting point of GB, SP, and Yang Wei Mai. Located one rib below LV-14 (Front-Mu of Liver) — the Mu points of the paired Liver and Gallbladder are adjacent. Ri (Sun) and Yue (Moon) = Yang (Gallbladder) and Yin (Liver), reflecting the pairing. Used to diagnose and treat Gallbladder disorders, especially excess/acute conditions.
memoryTrick: Sun and Moon = Ri (Sun/Yang) and Yue (Moon/Yin) represent the Gallbladder and Liver pairing. The Mu point of GB sits one rib below the Mu point of LV. The sun and moon are together on the ribcage.
cautions: Do not puncture deeply — risk of hepatomegaly or pneumothorax
specialCategories: Meeting point of GB, SP, Yang Wei Mai

name: Jing Men
location: On the lateral side of the abdomen, at the lower border of the free end of the twelfth rib
boardPearl: Front-Mu point of the Kidney (NOT of the Gallbladder). This is a commonly tested board fact — GB-25 on the Gallbladder channel serves as the Front-Mu of the Kidney. Located at the free end of the twelfth (floating) rib. The 'Capital Gate' is the gate to the Kidney — the Kidney is the capital of Yin.
memoryTrick: Capital Gate = the gate to the capital (Kidney). The Kidney is the 'capital' of the body's resources. A GB point being the Mu of the Kidney is a common board trap. At the tip of the 12th floating rib.
cautions: Avoid puncturing the kidney in thin patients

name: Dai Mai
location: On the lateral side of the abdomen, directly below LV-13, at the level of the umbilicus, at the crossing point of the vertical line through the free end of the eleventh rib and the horizontal line through the umbilicus
categories: Meeting point of GB and Dai Mai
boardPearl: Meeting point of GB and the Dai Mai (Girdling Vessel). This point is named after the Dai Mai itself. The Dai Mai encircles the waist like a belt and binds all the vertical channels. GB-26 is a key point for Dai Mai pathology: leucorrhea, vaginal discharge, irregular periods, and abdominal/lumbar pain. The Dai Mai circuit includes GB-26, GB-27, and GB-28.
memoryTrick: Girdling Vessel = the point IS the Dai Mai — named after the vessel it activates. The belt (Dai) wraps around the waist here. Think: 'belt at the waist treats what leaks below the belt.'
specialCategories: Meeting point of GB, Dai Mai

name: Wu Shu
location: On the lateral side of the abdomen, anterior to the superior iliac spine, 3 cun below the level of the umbilicus
categories: Meeting point of GB and Dai Mai
boardPearl: Meeting point of GB and Dai Mai. Part of the Dai Mai circuit (GB-26, GB-27, GB-28). Treats leucorrhea and lower abdominal/hernia disorders. The 'Five Pivots' may refer to the five Zang organs that pivot around the Dai Mai.
memoryTrick: Five Pivots = the five Zang pivot around the Dai Mai belt. One of the three Dai Mai points on the GB channel.
specialCategories: Meeting point of GB, Dai Mai

name: Wei Dao
location: On the lateral side of the abdomen, anterior and inferior to the anterior superior iliac spine, 0.5 cun anterior and inferior to GB-27
categories: Meeting point of GB and Dai Mai
boardPearl: Meeting point of GB and Dai Mai. The third and lowest point of the Dai Mai circuit on the GB channel. 'Linking Path' refers to the connection of the Dai Mai linking all the longitudinal channels.
memoryTrick: Linking Path = the Dai Mai links (connects) all the vertical pathways. The last of the three GB Dai Mai points (GB-26, 27, 28).
specialCategories: Meeting point of GB, Dai Mai

name: Ju Liao
location: On the hip, at the midpoint between the anterior superior iliac spine and the prominence of the greater trochanter
categories: Meeting point of GB and Yang Qiao Mai
indications: Pain of the lumbar region and thigh
boardPearl: Meeting point of GB and Yang Qiao Mai. Located midway between the ASIS and the greater trochanter. Important local point for hip disorders. The name 'Squatting Bone-Hole' refers to the depression at the hip found when squatting.
memoryTrick: Squatting Bone-Hole = when you squat, you can find the bone-hole (depression) at the hip. The hip flexion creates the depression.
specialCategories: Meeting point of GB, Yang Qiao Mai

name: Huan Tiao
location: On the buttock, at the junction of the lateral third and medial two thirds of the distance between the prominence of the greater trochanter and the sacral hiatus (GV-2), with the patient lying on their side with the thigh flexed
categories: Meeting point of GB and UB
Ma Dan Yang Heavenly Star point
indications: Pain in the lumbar region and thigh
Muscular atrophy of the lower limbs
Pain and numbness of the hip and leg
Skin diseases of the lower body
boardPearl: Meeting point of GB and UB. Ma Dan Yang Heavenly Star point. THE point for sciatica — possibly the single most important point for hip and posterior leg pain. Deep needling to obtain De Qi is essential. Located at the junction of the lateral 1/3 and medial 2/3 between greater trochanter and sacral hiatus. Patient should lie on their side with thigh flexed. Treats both GB and UB channel leg pain (since it is a meeting of both).
memoryTrick: Jumping Round = to jump, you need healthy hips. If you can't 'jump round' (circle/tiao), this point treats the hip. Also: the patient lies in a rounded position to access this deep point. Think: 'can't jump? GB-30.'
cautions: Deep point — needle carefully to avoid the sciatic nerve (although obtaining a sensation radiating down the leg is desirable)

name: Feng Shi
location: On the lateral aspect of the thigh, in the depression posterior to the iliotibial band where the tip of the middle finger rests when the patient stands erect with the arms hanging at the sides, 7 cun above the transverse popliteal crease
indications: Pain and weakness of the lower extremities
boardPearl: The 'Wind Market' is where Wind is bought and sold — the primary point for Wind affecting the skin (itching, urticaria, wind-rash). Found by standing with arms at the sides and noting where the tip of the middle finger touches the thigh. This location method is unique and commonly tested. Not a Five Shu or Luo point, but extremely important clinically for skin Wind conditions.
memoryTrick: Wind Market = the market/marketplace where Wind gathers on the leg. To find it, stand and let your middle finger drop — it naturally points to the Wind Market. Think: 'itchy skin? Go shopping at the Wind Market.'

name: Zhong Du
location: On the lateral aspect of the thigh, 5 cun above the transverse popliteal crease, between the vastus lateralis and the biceps femoris muscles, 2 cun below GB-31
indications: Pain and numbness of the lateral aspect of the thigh and knee
Atrophy and weakness of the lower extremities
boardPearl: Do not confuse GB-32 Zhong Du with LV-6 Zhong Du — same pinyin name but different characters and channels. GB-32 is on the lateral thigh; LV-6 is on the medial leg (Xi-Cleft of Liver).
memoryTrick: Central River = a river flowing centrally through the lateral thigh. Remember there are two 'Zhong Du' points: GB-32 (thigh) and LV-6 (leg, Xi-Cleft of Liver).

name: Xi Yang Guan
location: On the lateral aspect of the knee, in the depression above the lateral epicondyle of the femur, between the femur and the tendon of the biceps femoris, 3 cun above GB-34
indications: Pain and swelling of the knee
Contracture of the tendons in the popliteal fossa
boardPearl: Knee Yang Gate is a local point for lateral knee pain. Despite having 'Xi' in the name, this is NOT a Xi-Cleft point — the Xi-Cleft of the Gallbladder is GB-36 Wai Qiu. This is a common board trap.
memoryTrick: Knee Yang Gate = the Yang gate at the knee (lateral side = Yang). Do NOT confuse with Xi-Cleft despite the 'Xi' in the name. GB-36 is the real Xi-Cleft.

name: Yang Ling Quan
location: On the lateral aspect of the lower leg, in the depression anterior and inferior to the head of the fibula
categories: Ma Dan Yang Heavenly Star point
indications: Pain and weakness of the lower extremities
Sinew and tendon disorders anywhere in the body
boardPearl: He-Sea/Earth point of the Gallbladder channel. Influential (Hui) point of Sinews — treats ALL tendon and sinew disorders anywhere in the body. Ma Dan Yang Heavenly Star point. One of the most important points in acupuncture. The He-Sea point treats disorders of the fu organ (Gallbladder), making this essential for cholecystitis, jaundice, and bitter taste. As the Influential point of Sinews, it is used for any tendinitis, muscle spasm, or sinew contracture in the entire body. Compare with LV-8 (He-Sea of Liver, on the medial knee) — Yang Ling Quan is lateral.
memoryTrick: Yang Mound Spring = a spring rising from the Yang (lateral) mound (fibular head). The fibular head is the 'mound.' This is the SINEW point — think of the GB governing sinews and tendons. 'If it's a tendon problem anywhere, go to Yang Ling Quan.' Eight Influential Points mnemonic: sinews = GB-34.

name: Yang Jiao
location: On the lateral aspect of the lower leg, 7 cun above the tip of the lateral malleolus, on the posterior border of the fibula
indications: Fullness and distension of the chest and hypochondrium
Pain and swelling of the knee
boardPearl: Xi-Cleft point of the Yang Wei Mai (NOT of the Gallbladder channel). This is a commonly tested distinction: GB-35 is the Xi-Cleft of Yang Wei Mai; GB-36 is the Xi-Cleft of the Gallbladder channel itself. Located on the posterior border of the fibula, 7 cun above the lateral malleolus.
memoryTrick: Yang Intersection = the intersection of Yang channels — this is where the Yang Wei Mai's Xi-Cleft is located. Remember: GB-35 = Xi of Yang Wei; GB-36 = Xi of GB.

name: Wai Qiu
location: On the lateral aspect of the lower leg, 7 cun above the tip of the lateral malleolus, on the anterior border of the fibula
indications: Fullness and pain of the chest and hypochondrium
boardPearl: Xi-Cleft point of the Gallbladder channel. Xi-Cleft points treat acute conditions and pain. For Yang channels, Xi-Cleft points treat acute pain along the channel. GB-36 is at the same level as GB-35 (7 cun above lateral malleolus) but on the ANTERIOR border of the fibula (GB-35 is posterior). Key point for acute Gallbladder pain (cholecystitis, biliary colic).
memoryTrick: Outer Hill = the outer (lateral) hill (fibula). The Xi-Cleft of the Gallbladder is on the outer hill of the leg. GB-36 is ANTERIOR on the fibula, GB-35 is POSTERIOR — both at 7 cun above the malleolus.

name: Guang Ming
location: On the lateral aspect of the lower leg, 5 cun above the tip of the lateral malleolus, on the anterior border of the fibula
indications: Atrophy and weakness of the lower extremities
boardPearl: Luo-Connecting point of the Gallbladder channel. Connects to the Liver channel. The name 'Bright Light' reveals its primary function — treating eye disorders. The Gallbladder and Liver channels both relate to the eyes (Liver opens to the eyes). As the Luo point, GB-37 can treat both Gallbladder and Liver channel disorders. Key point for all visual disturbances including night blindness and blurred vision.
memoryTrick: Bright Light = this point brings bright light to the eyes. The Luo-Connecting point bridges GB to LV, and the Liver opens to the eyes. Think: 'when the light is dim (night blindness), use Guang Ming (Bright Light).'

name: Yang Fu
location: On the lateral aspect of the lower leg, 4 cun above the tip of the lateral malleolus, on the anterior border of the fibula, slightly anterior to GB-36
indications: Pain of the chest, hypochondrium, and lateral aspect of the lower extremities
boardPearl: Jing-River/Fire point and Sedation point of the Gallbladder channel. Fire is the child of Wood — sedation point (drain the child to sedate the mother). Jing-River points treat cough, asthma, and disorders of the voice/throat. As the Sedation point, GB-38 drains excess from the Gallbladder channel. Used for excess lateral headache and hypochondriac pain.
memoryTrick: Yang Assistance = this point assists the Yang (GB) channel by sedating its excess. Fire is the child of Wood: drain the Fire (child) to reduce Wood (Gallbladder). Think: 'too much Yang? GB-38 assists by sedating.'

name: Xuan Zhong
location: On the lateral aspect of the lower leg, 3 cun above the tip of the lateral malleolus, in the depression between the posterior border of the fibula and the tendons of the peroneus longus and brevis muscles
categories: Meeting point of the three Foot Yang channels (GB, ST, UB)
boardPearl: Influential (Hui) point of Marrow. Also known as Jue Gu (Severed Bone). Meeting point of the three Foot Yang channels (GB, ST, UB). Critical for all Marrow disorders — the brain is the 'Sea of Marrow,' so this point is essential for stroke, hemiplegia, dementia, and poor memory. Also treats bone disorders since Marrow fills the bones. One of the Eight Influential Points. The three Foot Yang channels meeting here makes it important for leg disorders. Key point for nourishing the brain (Sea of Marrow).
memoryTrick: Suspended Bell = a bell (zhong) hangs (xuan) at the ankle — the lateral malleolus looks like a suspended bell. Also called Jue Gu (Severed/Prominent Bone). For the Eight Influential Points: 'Marrow = GB-39.' When the brain (Sea of Marrow) is affected (stroke, dementia), use the Marrow point.

name: Qiu Xu
location: On the lateral side of the foot, anterior and inferior to the lateral malleolus, in the depression lateral to the tendon of the extensor digitorum longus
indications: Swelling and pain of the axilla
Pain and swelling of the lateral malleolus
boardPearl: Yuan-Source point of the Gallbladder channel. Yuan-Source points access the original Qi of their organ and are used to diagnose and treat organ disorders. GB-40 is important for Gallbladder and lateral ankle pain. As a Yuan-Source point, it can be paired with the Luo-Connecting point of the paired channel (LV-5) for interior-exterior treatment. Located in the depression anterior-inferior to the lateral malleolus.
memoryTrick: Mound of Ruins = the mound (malleolus) with ruins (depression) below it. The lateral malleolus is the mound, and the Yuan-Source point sits in the ruined (depressed) area below it. Think: 'the source (Yuan) of the Gallbladder springs from the ruins of the ankle mound.'

name: Zu Lin Qi
location: On the dorsum of the foot, in the depression distal to the junction of the fourth and fifth metatarsal bones, on the lateral side of the tendon of the extensor digiti minimi
indications: Swelling and pain of the dorsum of the foot
boardPearl: Shu-Stream/Wood point and Confluent point of the Dai Mai (Girdling Vessel). Paired with SJ-5 (Waiguan) — together they open the Dai Mai and treat lateral headache, ear, and eye disorders. As the Confluent point of Dai Mai, GB-41 treats all Dai Mai pathology: leucorrhea, irregular menstruation, hip pain, and disorders of the belt region. The Wood point on the Wood channel is the Horary point. As a Shu-Stream point on a Yang channel, it treats heaviness and joint pain.
memoryTrick: Foot Governor of Tears = governs lacrimation on the foot. Zu (foot) distinguishes it from GB-15 Tou Lin Qi (Head). Confluent of Dai Mai — pair with SJ-5. Think: 'tears from the foot (Zu Lin Qi) + outside gate (SJ-5) = Dai Mai opens, belt loosens.'

name: Di Wu Hui
location: On the dorsum of the foot, between the fourth and fifth metatarsal bones, in the depression proximal to the fourth metatarsophalangeal joint
indications: Pain and swelling of the axilla
Redness and swelling of the eyes
Pain and swelling of the dorsum of the foot
boardPearl: Located between the 4th and 5th metatarsals, proximal to the MTP joint. Treats breast and axillary pain along the Gallbladder channel pathway. Not commonly a primary exam point.
memoryTrick: Earth Fivefold Convergence = five paths converge on the earth (ground/foot). Located between the metatarsals where the GB channel approaches the toes.
cautions: Contraindicated for moxibustion according to some classical texts

name: Xia Xi
location: On the dorsum of the foot, between the fourth and fifth toes, proximal to the margin of the web, at the junction of the red and white skin
indications: Pain and swelling of the cheek
boardPearl: Ying-Spring/Water point and Tonification point of the Gallbladder channel. Water is the mother of Wood — tonification point (tonify the mother to strengthen the child/Wood). Ying-Spring points clear heat from the channel. This is a key point for clearing heat from the Gallbladder channel: headache, tinnitus, and febrile disease. Located in the web between the 4th and 5th toes.
memoryTrick: Clamped Stream = a stream clamped (squeezed) between the 4th and 5th toes. Water tonifies Wood (mother-child) making this the Tonification point. Think: 'water the wood from the clamped stream between the toes.'

name: Zu Qiao Yin
location: On the lateral side of the fourth toe, 0.1 cun proximal to the corner of the toenail
depth: 0.1 cun shallowly, or prick to bleed
categories: Exit point of the Gallbladder channel
boardPearl: Jing-Well/Metal point and Exit point of the Gallbladder channel. Jing-Well points are used for emergencies, loss of consciousness, and to clear heat from the opposite end of the channel. GB-44 particularly treats dream-disturbed sleep and insomnia — the Gallbladder controls the sleep/wake cycle through its role in decision-making and courage. The name 'Foot Portal Yin' contrasts with GB-11 'Head Portal Yin.' As a Well point, it can be bled for acute conditions.
memoryTrick: Foot Portal Yin = the portal (opening) to Yin at the foot. The endpoint of the Yang Gallbladder channel transitions to Yin (Liver) via the portal. Compare: GB-11 = Head Qiao Yin, GB-44 = Foot Qiao Yin. Think: 'the final portal from Yang to Yin at the foot.'

name: Da Dun
location: On the lateral side of the dorsal aspect of the big toe, 0.1 cun proximal to the corner of the toenail
depth: 0.1-0.2 cun shallowly, or prick to bleed
boardPearl: Jing-Well/Wood point of the Liver channel. Entry point of the Liver channel. The Liver channel wraps around the external genitalia, making LV-1 a key distal point for hernia (shan disorder) and genital disorders. As a Well point, it treats emergencies and loss of consciousness. The Wood point on the Wood channel makes it the Horary point. Treats uterine bleeding and prolapse due to Liver Qi not holding. Frequently tested for its role in treating hernia/shan disorder.
memoryTrick: Great Esteem = esteem for the great toe where the Liver channel begins. The 'great' (Da) toe holds this point of 'esteem' (Dun). Think: 'great toe, great for hernia and genital issues.'

name: Xing Jian
location: On the dorsum of the foot, between the first and second toes, proximal to the margin of the web, at the junction of the red and white skin
boardPearl: Ying-Spring/Fire point and Sedation point of the Liver channel. Fire is the child of Wood — sedation point (drain the child to sedate the mother). THE point for clearing Liver Fire blazing upward. Ying-Spring points clear heat from the channel, and combined with being the Fire point, LV-2 is the single most powerful point for draining Liver Fire. Treats red eyes, headache, irritability, insomnia, and hypertension from Liver Fire. Compare LV-2 (Sedation/Fire, for excess/fire) with LV-3 (Yuan-Source, for Qi stagnation).
memoryTrick: Moving Between = fire moves between the first and second toes. This Fire point on the Liver sedates excess Liver Fire. Think: 'if the Liver fire is BLAZING (excess), use LV-2 to SEDATE the fire that moves between the toes.'

name: Tai Chong
location: On the dorsum of the foot, in the depression distal to the junction of the first and second metatarsal bones
boardPearl: Shu-Stream/Earth point AND Yuan-Source point of the Liver channel. One of the MOST IMPORTANT points in all of acupuncture. THE point for Liver Qi stagnation — the single most common pattern in clinical practice. Combined with LI-4 (Hegu), they form the 'Four Gates' (Si Guan) — the most powerful combination for moving Qi and calming the spirit. As the Yuan-Source point, it accesses the Liver's original Qi and treats virtually all Liver disorders. Smooths Liver Qi, calms the spirit, clears the head, regulates menstruation, and subdues Liver Yang. The name 'Great Surge' refers to the surging of Liver Qi (and the palpable dorsalis pedis artery).
memoryTrick: Great Surge = the great surging of Liver Qi. When Qi is stuck (stagnation), this point creates a great surge to move it. LV-3 + LI-4 = Four Gates (Si Guan) = the ultimate Qi-moving combo. Think: 'when life is stagnant, let the Great Surge flow through the Four Gates.'

name: Zhong Feng
location: On the medial side of the ankle, in the depression medial to the tendon of the tibialis anterior, midway between SP-5 and ST-41, 1 cun anterior to the medial malleolus
boardPearl: Jing-River/Metal point of the Liver channel. Jing-River points treat cough, asthma, and voice/throat disorders. Located 1 cun anterior to the medial malleolus in the depression medial to the tibialis anterior tendon. Important for urogenital disorders (hernia, urinary retention) due to the Liver channel's course around the genitalia. Metal controls Wood — the Metal point on the Wood channel has a restraining/controlling effect.
memoryTrick: Mound Center = the center of the mound (medial malleolus). The ankle mound has a depression at its center where this point lies. Think: 'center of the ankle mound, centered between SP-5 and ST-41.'

name: Li Gou
location: On the medial aspect of the leg, 5 cun above the tip of the medial malleolus, on the medial surface of the tibia
depth: 0.5-1 cun perpendicularly along the posterior border of the tibia
indications: Pain in the medial aspect of the leg
boardPearl: Luo-Connecting point of the Liver channel. Connects to the Gallbladder channel. As the Luo point, it can treat disorders of both the Liver and Gallbladder channels. Particularly important for urogenital and menstrual disorders. The Liver channel wraps around the external genitalia, and LV-5 as the Luo point is especially effective for genital itching, leucorrhea, and urinary problems. Can be paired with GB-40 (Yuan-Source of GB) for interior-exterior treatment.
memoryTrick: Woodworm Canal = a canal (passage) eaten by woodworms (parasites). The Luo-Connecting point creates a canal linking Liver to Gallbladder. Think: 'the worm canal connects Wood (Liver) to its pair.'

name: Zhong Du
location: On the medial aspect of the leg, 7 cun above the tip of the medial malleolus, on the medial surface of the tibia, posterior to the medial border of the tibia
boardPearl: Xi-Cleft point of the Liver channel. Xi-Cleft points treat acute conditions and pain. For Yin channels, Xi-Cleft points especially treat BLOOD disorders — LV-6 treats uterine bleeding, prolonged lochia, and acute hernia pain. Do not confuse with GB-32 Zhong Du (same pinyin, different characters) — LV-6 is on the medial leg, GB-32 is on the lateral thigh. The Xi-Cleft of the Liver is crucial for acute Liver blood stasis causing uterine hemorrhage.
memoryTrick: Central Capital = the capital (center) of the Liver's Xi-Cleft emergency. When there is acute bleeding (the Liver stores blood), rush to the Central Capital for help. Two Zhong Du points: LV-6 (medial leg, Xi-Cleft) and GB-32 (lateral thigh).

name: Xi Guan
location: On the medial aspect of the leg, 1 cun posterior to SP-9, in the depression posterior and inferior to the medial condyle of the tibia
indications: Pain and swelling of the medial aspect of the knee
Pain in the medial aspect of the leg
boardPearl: Local point for medial knee pain. Located 1 cun posterior to SP-9 (Yinlingquan). The name 'Knee Gate' directly indicates its use: the gate (guan) of the knee (xi). Not a Five Shu or special category point, but useful as a local point for knee osteoarthritis and medial knee disorders.
memoryTrick: Knee Gate = the gate to healing the knee. Located just behind SP-9 at the medial knee. Think: 'open the knee gate to relieve knee pain.'

name: Qu Quan
location: On the medial aspect of the knee, at the medial end of the transverse popliteal crease, in the depression anterior to the tendons of the semitendinosus and semimembranosus muscles, posterior to the medial epicondyle of the femur
indications: Pain in the medial aspect of the knee
boardPearl: He-Sea/Water point and Tonification point of the Liver channel. Water is the mother of Wood — tonification point (tonify the mother to nourish the child/Wood). He-Sea points treat diarrhea and disorders of the fu organs. As the Water point, LV-8 nourishes Liver Yin and Blood — essential for Liver Blood deficiency, Liver Yin deficiency, and Kidney/Liver Yin deficiency. Compare: LV-2 (Sedation/Fire, for excess) vs. LV-8 (Tonification/Water, for deficiency). Located at the medial end of the popliteal crease when the knee is flexed. Counterpart to GB-34 (He-Sea of GB on the lateral knee).
memoryTrick: Spring at the Crook = a water spring in the crook (bend) of the knee. Water tonifies Wood (mother feeds child). Think: 'when the Liver is DRY and DEFICIENT, water it from the spring at the crook of the knee.' LV-8 for deficiency, LV-2 for excess.

name: Yin Bao
location: On the medial aspect of the thigh, 4 cun above the medial epicondyle of the femur, between the vastus medialis and the sartorius muscles
boardPearl: Located on the medial thigh, 4 cun above the medial epicondyle of the femur. 'Yin Wrapping' reflects the Liver channel's course through the Yin (medial) aspect of the thigh toward the genitalia. Treats menstrual and urinary disorders. Not commonly a primary exam point.
memoryTrick: Yin Wrapping = the Yin wraps around the inner thigh. The Liver channel wraps the medial (Yin) aspect of the thigh en route to the genitalia.

name: Zu Wu Li
location: On the medial aspect of the thigh, 3 cun below ST-30, on the anterior border of the adductor longus muscle
indications: Pain and itching of the genitalia
boardPearl: Located on the medial thigh near the inguinal region. Do not confuse with LI-13 (Arm/Hand Wu Li) — same name concept but different location. 'Foot Five Li' is on the leg; 'Hand Five Li' (LI-13) is on the arm. Treats local genital and inguinal problems.
memoryTrick: Foot Five Li = five li (Chinese distance unit) on the foot/leg. Distinguishes it from the arm version (LI-13). Located on the medial thigh near the groin.
cautions: Contraindicated for moxibustion in some classical texts

name: Yin Lian
location: On the medial aspect of the thigh, 2 cun below ST-30, at the anterior border of the adductor longus muscle
boardPearl: Located on the medial thigh near the inguinal region, 2 cun below ST-30. The 'Yin Corner' is where the Yin (medial) aspect of the thigh meets the groin. A local point for medial thigh pain and menstrual disorders. Not commonly a primary exam point.
memoryTrick: Yin Corner = the corner where Yin (inner) thigh meets the body at the groin. A boundary point at the inner corner of the leg and trunk.
cautions: Avoid the femoral artery and vein

name: Ji Mai
location: On the inguinal region, inferior and lateral to the pubic spine, 2.5 cun lateral to the anterior midline, in the inguinal groove lateral to the femoral artery
boardPearl: Located at the inguinal groove where the Liver channel passes near the external genitalia. The 'Urgent Pulse' refers to the palpable femoral artery at this location. Important local point for hernia and genital pain due to the Liver channel's course around the genitalia. The Liver channel enters the lower abdomen and wraps around the genitalia — LV-12 is right at this transition.
memoryTrick: Urgent Pulse = the urgent (pounding) pulse of the femoral artery at the groin. Feel the urgent pulse to locate this point at the inguinal groove.
cautions: Avoid the femoral artery and femoral nerve

name: Zhang Men
location: On the lateral side of the abdomen, below the free end of the eleventh floating rib
categories: Influential point of Zang organs (Hui-Meeting point)
Meeting point of LV and GB
boardPearl: Front-Mu point of the Spleen AND Influential (Hui) point of the Zang (solid) organs. Meeting point of LV and GB. A triple-function point — incredibly important for boards. As the Front-Mu of the Spleen, it diagnoses and treats Spleen disorders (especially excess/acute). As the Influential point of Zang organs, it treats disorders of ANY solid organ (Heart, Lung, Spleen, Liver, Kidney). The fact that a Liver channel point is the Mu of the Spleen reflects the Wood-Earth (Liver-Spleen) relationship — Liver controls (ke cycle) the Spleen. Located at the free end of the 11th rib. Eight Influential Points mnemonic: Zang = LV-13.
memoryTrick: Camphorwood Gate = the gate of the camphorwood (zhang) tree. Wood (Liver) controls the gate to Earth (Spleen). LV-13 on the Liver channel is the Mu of the Spleen — Wood controls Earth. Think: 'the Liver (Wood) opens the gate to the Spleen.' Also: Zang organs' Influential point = 'Zhang Men' sounds like 'Zang' — Zhang Men for Zang organs.
cautions: Do not puncture deeply in thin patients — risk of puncturing the Spleen or Liver

name: Qi Men
location: On the chest, directly below the nipple, in the sixth intercostal space, 4 cun lateral to the anterior midline
categories: Meeting point of LV, SP, and Yin Wei Mai
Exit point of the Liver channel
boardPearl: Front-Mu point of the Liver. Meeting point of LV, SP, and Yin Wei Mai. Exit point of the Liver channel. The last point on the Liver channel — the Liver channel is the last of the 12 channels, and LV-14 is its final point. 'Cycle Gate' refers to the completion of the cycle of Qi through all 12 channels: the Qi cycle begins at LU-1 (Zhong Fu) and ends at LV-14 (Qi Men), then returns to LU-1. As the Front-Mu of the Liver, it diagnoses and treats Liver disorders. Located one rib above GB-24 (Front-Mu of GB) — the Mu points of the paired Liver and Gallbladder are adjacent on the ribcage.
memoryTrick: Cycle Gate = the gate (Men) where the Qi cycle (Qi) completes. The full cycle of 12 channels ends here. Think: 'the Qi (cycle) passes through this final gate (Men) before starting a new cycle at the Lungs.' Also: LV-14 is one rib ABOVE GB-24 — Liver above Gallbladder on the chest.
cautions: Do not puncture deeply — risk of pneumothorax or puncturing the liver
specialCategories: Meeting point of LV, SP, Yin Wei Mai

name: Jing Ming
location: In the depression slightly above the inner canthus of the eye, near the medial border of the orbit.
depth: 0.3-0.5 cun perpendicular along the orbital wall, pushing the eyeball laterally. Slow insertion, no manipulation.
categories: Meeting of UB, SI, ST, GB, Yin Qiao Mai, Yang Qiao Mai
indications: redness and pain of the eye
boardPearl: Meeting point of UB, SI, ST, GB, Yin Qiao Mai, and Yang Qiao Mai — the point where the most channels converge (6 channels). Major point for ALL eye disorders. Starting point of the Bladder channel.
memoryTrick: Jing Ming = 'Bright Eyes' — the eye point that brings brightness. 6 channels meet here, like 6 rays of light converging at the eye.
cautions: No retention, no manipulation. Press firmly after withdrawal to prevent hematoma. Contraindicated for moxibustion.
specialCategories: Meeting point of UB, SI, ST, GB, Yin Qiao Mai, Yang Qiao Mai

name: Zan Zhu
location: At the medial end of the eyebrow, directly above the inner canthus, in the supraorbital notch.
depth: 0.3-0.5 cun transverse insertion directed laterally along the eyebrow, or perpendicular 0.3 cun.
indications: pain and redness of the eye
boardPearl: Located at the supraorbital notch — important for frontal headache and supraorbital neuralgia. Excellent local point for eye disorders alongside UB-1.
memoryTrick: Zan Zhu = 'Gathered Bamboo' — eyebrow hairs cluster together like bamboo at the medial end.
cautions: Avoid deep perpendicular insertion to prevent entering the orbit.

name: Mei Chong
location: Directly above UB-2, 0.5 cun within the anterior hairline, between Du-24 and UB-4.
boardPearl: Located 0.5 cun within the anterior hairline, directly above UB-2. Rarely tested but know the location for its relationship to other head points.
memoryTrick: Mei Chong = 'Eyebrow Ascension' — it ascends (chong = surge upward) directly above the eyebrow (mei).

name: Qu Cha
location: 0.5 cun within the anterior hairline, 1.5 cun lateral to the midline (Du-24).
boardPearl: 1.5 cun lateral to Du-24 and 0.5 cun within the anterior hairline. Marks the 1.5-cun lateral line of the head where the Bladder channel runs.
memoryTrick: Qu Cha = 'Deviating Turn' — the channel deviates (turns) from the midline here, branching 1.5 cun laterally.

name: Wu Chu
location: 1 cun directly posterior to UB-4, 1.5 cun lateral to the midline, 1 cun posterior to the anterior hairline.
boardPearl: The 'Fifth Place' — 5th point on the Bladder channel. Located 1 cun posterior to the anterior hairline, 1.5 cun lateral to Du Mai.
memoryTrick: Wu Chu = 'Fifth Place' — the 5th point on the UB channel, so its name literally tells you its number.

name: Cheng Guang
location: 1.5 cun posterior to UB-5, 2.5 cun posterior to the anterior hairline, 1.5 cun lateral to the midline.
boardPearl: Located 2.5 cun posterior to the anterior hairline on the 1.5-cun line. 'Receiving Light' reflects its use for visual disorders.
memoryTrick: Cheng Guang = 'Receiving Light' — the scalp receives sunlight here; used for eye/vision problems.

name: Tong Tian
location: 1.5 cun posterior to UB-6, 4 cun posterior to the anterior hairline, 1.5 cun lateral to the midline.
boardPearl: Window of Heaven point. Important for nasal obstruction and rhinitis. 'Tong Tian' means connecting to Heaven — the nose connects the body to the Heavens (air/qi).
memoryTrick: Tong Tian = 'Celestial Connection' — a Window of Heaven point that opens the nose (our connection to heaven's air). Think: 'Tongue (Tong) to Heaven (Tian)' = breathe through the nose.

name: Luo Que
location: 1.5 cun posterior to UB-7, 5.5 cun posterior to the anterior hairline, 1.5 cun lateral to the midline.
boardPearl: Located 5.5 cun posterior to the anterior hairline. The name 'Declining Connection' refers to the channel declining downward from here toward the nape.
memoryTrick: Luo Que = 'Declining Connection' — the channel starts to decline (descend) from the vertex toward the nape.

name: Yu Zhen
location: 1.3 cun lateral to the midline, level with the upper border of the external occipital protuberance (Du-17), 2.5 cun posterior to UB-8.
boardPearl: Located at the occiput level with Du-17. 'Jade Pillow' — the occipital bone is the jade pillow of the head. Important for occipital headaches.
memoryTrick: Yu Zhen = 'Jade Pillow' — the back of the head rests on a pillow; this point is on the occiput where the head meets the pillow.

name: Tian Zhu
location: On the nape, 1.3 cun lateral to the midline (Du-15), within the posterior hairline, in the depression on the lateral aspect of the trapezius muscle.
depth: 0.5-0.8 cun perpendicular or slightly inferiorly. Do not puncture deeply in a superior direction.
boardPearl: Window of Heaven point and Sea of Qi point. One of the most important points on the upper back/nape for stiff neck, occipital headache, and stress. The 'pillar' that supports heaven (the head).
memoryTrick: Tian Zhu = 'Celestial Pillar' — the neck is the pillar supporting the celestial (head/heaven). Window of Heaven — opens the senses. Two 'Tian' points on UB: Tian Zhu (UB-10) and Tong Tian (UB-7).
cautions: Do not needle deeply in an upward/medial direction toward the medulla oblongata.

name: Da Zhu
location: 1.5 cun lateral to the lower border of the spinous process of the 1st thoracic vertebra (T1).
categories: Meeting of UB, SI, GB, Du Mai
boardPearl: Influential (Hui) point of BONES — the #1 point for all bone disorders. Meeting point of UB, SI, GB, and Du Mai. Also called the 'Sea of Blood' point by some classics. At T1 level, 1.5 cun lateral.
memoryTrick: Da Zhu = 'Great Shuttle' — the great weaving shuttle of bones, like the shuttle of a loom creating the skeletal framework. 'Da' (great) + bones = the great bone point.
cautions: Oblique insertion to avoid pneumothorax.
specialCategories: Meeting of UB, SI, GB, Du Mai

name: Feng Men
location: 1.5 cun lateral to the lower border of the spinous process of the 2nd thoracic vertebra (T2).
categories: Meeting of UB and Du Mai
boardPearl: Wind Gate — the gate through which Wind enters and exits the body. Key point for expelling Wind, treating early-stage Wind-Cold invasion. At T2 level, often cupped or used with gua sha for Wind-Cold. Meeting of UB and Du Mai.
memoryTrick: Feng Men = 'Wind Gate' — the gate where Wind pathogen enters the body. Think: 'close the Wind Gate' with needling/cupping to expel Wind-Cold.
cautions: Oblique insertion to avoid pneumothorax.
specialCategories: Meeting of UB and Du Mai

name: Fei Shu
location: 1.5 cun lateral to the lower border of the spinous process of the 3rd thoracic vertebra (T3).
boardPearl: Back-Shu point of the Lung at T3. Essential for ALL Lung disorders — both excess (cough, asthma, phlegm) and deficiency (Lung Qi/Yin xu). First of the Back-Shu points. Remember: Lung = T3.
memoryTrick: Fei Shu at T3 — 'Free (Fei) to breathe at T3.' The Lung Shu is the first Back-Shu, just as the Lung is the first organ in the organ clock.
cautions: Oblique insertion to avoid pneumothorax.

name: Jue Yin Shu
location: 1.5 cun lateral to the lower border of the spinous process of the 4th thoracic vertebra (T4).
boardPearl: Back-Shu point of the Pericardium at T4. 'Jue Yin Shu' literally means 'Jue Yin transporting point' — referring to the Pericardium (Hand Jue Yin). T4 level.
memoryTrick: Jue Yin Shu at T4 — 'Jue Yin protects the heart at T-FOUR.' The Pericardium wraps and protects the Heart, and its Shu point is one level above the Heart Shu.
cautions: Oblique insertion to avoid pneumothorax.

name: Xin Shu
location: 1.5 cun lateral to the lower border of the spinous process of the 5th thoracic vertebra (T5).
boardPearl: Back-Shu point of the Heart at T5. Critical for all Heart disorders: palpitations, insomnia, anxiety, mania. Combined with UB-44 (Shen Tang) for spirit disorders. Heart = T5.
memoryTrick: Xin Shu at T5 — 'The Heart (Xin) is alive (5 = alive in Chinese, wu/sheng).' T5 = Heart Shu. Or: 'High FIVE for the Heart.'
cautions: Oblique insertion to avoid pneumothorax.

name: Du Shu
location: 1.5 cun lateral to the lower border of the spinous process of the 6th thoracic vertebra (T6).
categories: Back-Shu point of the Du Mai
boardPearl: Back-Shu of the Du Mai (Governing Vessel) at T6. Not a Zang-Fu Back-Shu per se. Located between Heart Shu (T5) and Ge Shu (T7). Know T6 = Du Shu.
memoryTrick: Du Shu at T6 — the Du Vessel's own Shu point lives at T-SIX. 'Du sits at six.'
cautions: Oblique insertion to avoid pneumothorax.
specialCategories: Back-Shu point of the Du Mai

name: Ge Shu
location: 1.5 cun lateral to the lower border of the spinous process of the 7th thoracic vertebra (T7).
boardPearl: Influential (Hui) point of BLOOD — the #1 point for ALL blood disorders (anemia, blood stasis, bleeding). At T7 (the dividing line between upper and lower Back-Shu points). Remember: 'Blood at 7' or 'Ge Shu governs the gore (blood).' Also Back-Shu of the Diaphragm — hiccups, difficulty swallowing.
memoryTrick: Ge Shu = 'Diaphragm Shu' at T7 — '7 pints of blood' or 'the diaphragm is lucky #7.' Influential of Blood: 'Go (Ge) get blood at 7.'
cautions: Oblique insertion to avoid pneumothorax.

name: Gan Shu
location: 1.5 cun lateral to the lower border of the spinous process of the 9th thoracic vertebra (T9).
boardPearl: Back-Shu point of the Liver at T9. Essential for all Liver disorders: Liver Qi stagnation, Liver Blood xu, Liver Yang rising, Liver Wind. Key for eye disorders (Liver opens to the eyes) and emotional disorders (depression/anger). T9 = Liver.
memoryTrick: Gan Shu at T9 — 'Liver (Gan) is fine (9 = fine/jiu)' or 'the Liver lives at T-NINE.' Note T8 is skipped (no standard Back-Shu at T8).
cautions: Oblique insertion to avoid pneumothorax.

name: Dan Shu
location: 1.5 cun lateral to the lower border of the spinous process of the 10th thoracic vertebra (T10).
boardPearl: Back-Shu point of the Gallbladder at T10. One level below Liver Shu (T9), reflecting their interior-exterior relationship. Key for damp-heat jaundice, bitter taste, and Gallbladder-related emotional issues (indecisiveness, timidity).
memoryTrick: Dan Shu at T10 — 'Gallbladder (Dan) makes decisions at TEN.' Liver (T9) and Gallbladder (T10) are neighbors, just like their paired relationship.
cautions: Oblique insertion to avoid pneumothorax.

name: Pi Shu
location: 1.5 cun lateral to the lower border of the spinous process of the 11th thoracic vertebra (T11).
boardPearl: Back-Shu point of the Spleen at T11. Essential for all Spleen disorders: Spleen Qi xu, Dampness, poor appetite, loose stools, edema, Spleen not holding Blood. Often combined with ST-36 and CV-12 for Spleen Qi deficiency. T11 = Spleen.
memoryTrick: Pi Shu at T11 — 'Spleen (Pi) lives at T-ELEVEN.' Think: the Spleen needs to 'pick itself up' (Pi = pick up) at 11 o'clock.
cautions: Oblique insertion. Less pneumothorax risk than upper thoracic points but still exercise care.

name: Wei Shu
location: 1.5 cun lateral to the lower border of the spinous process of the 12th thoracic vertebra (T12).
boardPearl: Back-Shu point of the Stomach at T12. For all Stomach disorders: Stomach Qi deficiency, Stomach Fire, Food stagnation, rebellious Stomach Qi (nausea/vomiting). T12 = Stomach. Spleen (T11) and Stomach (T12) are paired neighbors.
memoryTrick: Wei Shu at T12 — 'Stomach (Wei) waits at TWELVE.' T11 (Spleen) and T12 (Stomach) are paired, just like their organ relationship.
cautions: Oblique insertion.

name: San Jiao Shu
location: 1.5 cun lateral to the lower border of the spinous process of the 1st lumbar vertebra (L1).
categories: Back-Shu point of the San Jiao
indications: stiffness and pain of the lower back
boardPearl: Back-Shu point of the San Jiao at L1. Important for water metabolism disorders (edema, urinary difficulties) — San Jiao is the 'official of the water passages.' First Back-Shu point at the lumbar level. L1 = San Jiao.
memoryTrick: San Jiao Shu at L1 — 'San Jiao starts the Lumbar series at L-ONE.' The waterworks official governs the first lumbar.
specialCategories: Back-Shu point of the San Jiao

name: Shen Shu
location: 1.5 cun lateral to the lower border of the spinous process of the 2nd lumbar vertebra (L2).
boardPearl: Back-Shu point of the Kidney at L2. One of the most important and commonly used Back-Shu points. Essential for ALL Kidney disorders: Kidney Yin xu, Kidney Yang xu, Kidney Qi xu, Kidney Jing xu. Low back pain (any etiology), reproductive/urinary disorders, bone/hearing problems. L2 = Kidney.
memoryTrick: Shen Shu at L2 — 'Kidneys (Shen) are TWO (L2), just like we have TWO kidneys.' The Kidneys live at L2, which is also anatomically near the actual kidneys.

name: Qi Hai Shu
location: 1.5 cun lateral to the lower border of the spinous process of the 3rd lumbar vertebra (L3).
boardPearl: Located at L3. Named 'Sea of Qi Shu' as the posterior counterpart of CV-6 (Qi Hai). Reinforces Qi, especially lower back and menstrual disorders. Not a Zang-Fu Back-Shu point.
memoryTrick: Qi Hai Shu at L3 — 'Sea of Qi on the back at L3.' It corresponds to CV-6 (Qi Hai) on the front.

name: Da Chang Shu
location: 1.5 cun lateral to the lower border of the spinous process of the 4th lumbar vertebra (L4).
