categories: Back-Shu point of the Large Intestine
indications: muscular atrophy of the lower limbs
boardPearl: Back-Shu point of the Large Intestine at L4. Important for all Large Intestine disorders (diarrhea, constipation, dysentery) and low back pain/sciatica (L4 dermatome). L4 = Large Intestine.
memoryTrick: Da Chang Shu at L4 — 'Large Intestine (Da Chang) sits at L-FOUR.' Think: 'Da Chang goes on all FOURs' for L4.
specialCategories: Back-Shu point of the Large Intestine

name: Guan Yuan Shu
location: 1.5 cun lateral to the lower border of the spinous process of the 5th lumbar vertebra (L5).
boardPearl: Located at L5. Named 'Guan Yuan Shu' as the posterior counterpart of CV-4 (Guan Yuan). Not a Zang-Fu Back-Shu but reinforces the original Qi. Good for urinary and lower back disorders.
memoryTrick: Guan Yuan Shu at L5 — 'Origin Pass (Guan Yuan) on the back at L5.' Corresponds to CV-4 (Guan Yuan) on the front.

name: Xiao Chang Shu
location: 1.5 cun lateral to the midline, at the level of the 1st posterior sacral foramen.
categories: Back-Shu point of the Small Intestine
boardPearl: Back-Shu point of the Small Intestine, at the 1st sacral foramen level. Key for separating the pure from the impure (Small Intestine function). Used for urinary and intestinal disorders. Sacral level = S1.
memoryTrick: Xiao Chang Shu at S1 — 'Small Intestine (Xiao Chang) starts the sacral series.' The Small Intestine sorts things out at sacral level 1.
specialCategories: Back-Shu point of the Small Intestine

name: Pang Guang Shu
location: 1.5 cun lateral to the midline, at the level of the 2nd posterior sacral foramen.
categories: Back-Shu point of the Urinary Bladder
boardPearl: Back-Shu point of the Urinary Bladder itself, at the 2nd sacral foramen level (S2). The Bladder's own Back-Shu point — essential for all urinary disorders: retention, incontinence, cystitis. S2 = Bladder.
memoryTrick: Pang Guang Shu at S2 — 'The Bladder (Pang Guang) holds Number TWO at S2.' The Bladder's own Shu point is at its anatomical neighborhood.
specialCategories: Back-Shu point of the Urinary Bladder

name: Zhong Lu Shu
location: 1.5 cun lateral to the midline, at the level of the 3rd posterior sacral foramen.
boardPearl: Located at the 3rd sacral foramen level (S3). Not a Zang-Fu Back-Shu point. Used locally for sacral and low back pain. 'Zhong Lu' means 'middle of the backbone.'
memoryTrick: Zhong Lu Shu at S3 — 'Middle (Zhong) of the backbone (Lu) at S3.' It sits in the middle of the sacral points.

name: Bai Huan Shu
location: 1.5 cun lateral to the midline, at the level of the 4th posterior sacral foramen.
indications: prolapse of the uterus or rectum
boardPearl: Located at the 4th sacral foramen level (S4). Last point of the first (medial) Bladder line on the back. Important for gynecological disorders (leucorrhea — 'White Ring' = white discharge), prolapse, and sacral pain.
memoryTrick: Bai Huan Shu = 'White Ring Shu' at S4 — 'white ring' refers to leucorrhea (white vaginal discharge). Think: white ring around the sacral area at S4.

name: Shang Liao
location: In the 1st posterior sacral foramen, midway between the posterior superior iliac spine (PSIS) and the midline.
boardPearl: The 'Four Liao' points (UB-31 to UB-34) are in the four sacral foramina. Shang Liao (Upper Crevice) is in the 1st foramen. All four are critical for gynecological, urological, and sacral disorders. Commonly used together as a group.
memoryTrick: Shang Liao = 'Upper Crevice' — the uppermost (Shang) of the four sacral foramen points. 'S-L-C-X: Shang-Ci-Zhong-Xia = Upper-Second-Middle-Lower' for UB-31 to 34.

name: Ci Liao
location: In the 2nd posterior sacral foramen.
boardPearl: In the 2nd sacral foramen. Often considered the most important of the Four Liao for gynecological disorders, especially dysmenorrhea and irregular menstruation. Strong empirical point for labor induction.
memoryTrick: Ci Liao = 'Second Crevice' — Ci means second/next, in the 2nd sacral foramen. The most commonly used of the Four Liao.

name: Zhong Liao
location: In the 3rd posterior sacral foramen.
boardPearl: In the 3rd sacral foramen. 'Middle Crevice' — the middle of the four sacral foramen points. Used as part of the Ba Liao group for sacral, gynecological, and urological disorders.
memoryTrick: Zhong Liao = 'Middle Crevice' — Zhong means middle, in the 3rd (middle) sacral foramen.

name: Xia Liao
location: In the 4th posterior sacral foramen.
boardPearl: In the 4th sacral foramen. The lowest of the Four Liao. Together UB-31 to UB-34 form the 'Ba Liao' (Eight Crevices — 4 on each side). All four treat sacral, gynecological, and urological disorders.
memoryTrick: Xia Liao = 'Lower Crevice' — Xia means lower, in the 4th (lowest) sacral foramen. The Ba Liao = 8 holes (4 foramina x 2 sides).

name: Hui Yang
location: 0.5 cun lateral to the tip of the coccyx (Du-1).
boardPearl: Located 0.5 cun lateral to the coccyx tip (Du-1 level). 'Meeting of Yang' — where the Bladder Yang Qi meets the Du Mai (Sea of Yang). Important for hemorrhoids and anorectal disorders.
memoryTrick: Hui Yang = 'Meeting of Yang' — Yang meets at the tail end of the spine, next to the coccyx and Du-1.

name: Cheng Fu
location: At the midpoint of the inferior gluteal crease (transverse gluteal fold).
indications: muscular atrophy of the lower limbs
boardPearl: At the midpoint of the gluteal fold — easy landmark. Key point for sciatica (sciatic nerve runs deep here) and posterior thigh/gluteal pain. 'Support' — supports the weight of the body when sitting.
memoryTrick: Cheng Fu = 'Support' — the gluteal crease supports (holds up) the buttock. Think: 'the crease where you sit and are supported.'
cautions: Deep needling near the sciatic nerve — avoid direct needling of the nerve.

name: Yin Men
location: 6 cun below UB-36, on the posterior aspect of the thigh, on the line connecting UB-36 and UB-40.
indications: difficulty in flexing and extending the hip
boardPearl: Located 6 cun below UB-36 on the posterior thigh. Primarily a local point for sciatica and posterior thigh pain along the Bladder channel pathway.
memoryTrick: Yin Men = 'Gate of Abundance' — the gate to the rich (abundant) posterior thigh muscles (hamstrings).

name: Fu Xi
location: 1 cun above UB-39 (Wei Yang), on the medial side of the tendon of the biceps femoris muscle, on the lateral end of the popliteal crease.
indications: contracture of the tendons in the popliteal fossa
boardPearl: Located 1 cun above UB-39. A minor point. The 'Superficial Cleft' lies near the surface at the lateral popliteal region.
memoryTrick: Fu Xi = 'Superficial Cleft' — a shallow (superficial) depression (cleft) at the lateral popliteal area, 1 cun above Wei Yang.

name: Wei Yang
location: On the lateral end of the popliteal crease, medial to the tendon of the biceps femoris muscle.
categories: Lower He-Sea point of the San Jiao
indications: Stiffness and fullness of the lower abdomen
boardPearl: Lower He-Sea point of the San Jiao — one of the three Lower He-Sea points on the Bladder channel (UB-39 for SJ, UB-40 for UB itself). Used for San Jiao water metabolism disorders (edema, urinary difficulties). Located lateral to UB-40 in the popliteal crease.
memoryTrick: Wei Yang = 'Bend Yang' — at the lateral (Yang) side of the popliteal bend. Lower He-Sea of SJ: 'the Triple Burner's waterworks drains at the knee bend.'
specialCategories: Lower He-Sea point of the San Jiao

name: Wei Zhong
location: At the midpoint of the popliteal crease, between the tendons of the biceps femoris and semitendinosus muscles.
depth: 0.5-1.5 cun perpendicular. Or prick to bleed for Blood Heat/stasis patterns.
categories: Ma Dan Yang Heavenly Star point
Lower He-Sea point of the Bladder
boardPearl: He-Sea/Earth point. Ma Dan Yang Heavenly Star point. COMMAND POINT OF THE BACK — the single most important distal point for low back pain. Pricked to bleed for acute lumbar sprain, Blood Heat skin diseases, and summer-heat. Lower He-Sea of the Bladder itself. 'For the back, seek Wei Zhong.' One of the Four Command Points.
memoryTrick: Wei Zhong = 'Bend Middle' — the middle of the popliteal bend. 'For the back, seek Wei Zhong' — the #1 distal point for any back problem. Earth point of Water channel = the mud at the bottom of the river.
cautions: Avoid the popliteal artery and vein which lie deep in the midline of the popliteal fossa.
specialCategories: Ma Dan Yang Heavenly Star point
Lower He-Sea point of the Bladder

name: Fu Fen
location: 3 cun lateral to the lower border of the spinous process of the 2nd thoracic vertebra (T2).
indications: Stiffness and pain of the shoulder and back
numbness of the elbow and arm
boardPearl: First point of the second (outer) Bladder line, 3 cun lateral to the midline at T2 level (same level as UB-12 Feng Men). Meeting of UB and SI. Used for upper back/shoulder stiffness and pain.
memoryTrick: Fu Fen = 'Attached Branch' — the start of the branch (second line) that attaches alongside the first Bladder line. At T2, 3 cun lateral.
cautions: Oblique insertion to avoid pneumothorax.

name: Po Hu
location: 3 cun lateral to the lower border of the spinous process of the 3rd thoracic vertebra (T3).
indications: stiffness and pain of the shoulder and back
boardPearl: At T3 level (same as UB-13 Fei Shu). 'Door of the Po (Corporeal Soul)' — the Po resides in the Lung. Used for Lung disorders and grief/sadness affecting the Po. The outer Bladder line points relate to the spirit aspects of their corresponding organs.
memoryTrick: Po Hu = 'Door of the Po' — the Po (Corporeal Soul) lives in the Lung, and this point is at the Lung level (T3). 'Open the door to let the Po breathe.'
cautions: Oblique insertion to avoid pneumothorax.

name: Gao Huang Shu
location: 3 cun lateral to the lower border of the spinous process of the 4th thoracic vertebra (T4).
boardPearl: One of the most important points for CHRONIC DEFICIENCY and exhaustion. 'Gao Huang' refers to the region between the heart and diaphragm — disease that reaches the Gao Huang is considered incurable, but this point treats it. Often used with moxa for severe chronic deficiency of any type. At T4, the outer line counterpart of UB-14.
memoryTrick: Gao Huang Shu = 'Vital Region Shu' — when disease reaches the Gao Huang (vital region), it is hopeless... unless you needle/moxa this point! The 'last resort' point for deep chronic deficiency.
cautions: Oblique insertion to avoid pneumothorax.

name: Shen Tang
location: 3 cun lateral to the lower border of the spinous process of the 5th thoracic vertebra (T5).
boardPearl: At T5 level (same as UB-15 Xin Shu). 'Spirit Hall' — the Shen (Spirit) resides in the Heart. Used for Heart/Spirit disorders: insomnia, anxiety, palpitations. The outer line counterpart addresses the spirit aspect of the Heart.
memoryTrick: Shen Tang = 'Spirit Hall' — the hall where the Shen (spirit) dwells, at the Heart level (T5). 'The spirit lives in the Heart's hall.'
cautions: Oblique insertion to avoid pneumothorax.

name: Yi Xi
location: 3 cun lateral to the lower border of the spinous process of the 6th thoracic vertebra (T6).
boardPearl: At T6 level (same as UB-16 Du Shu). 'Yi Xi' is an exclamation of sighing. Used for chest/upper back pain and respiratory disorders.
memoryTrick: Yi Xi = the sound of a sigh — at T6, one might sigh from upper back stiffness and chest tightness.
cautions: Oblique insertion to avoid pneumothorax.

name: Ge Guan
location: 3 cun lateral to the lower border of the spinous process of the 7th thoracic vertebra (T7).
boardPearl: At T7 level (same as UB-17 Ge Shu). 'Diaphragm Pass' — the outer line counterpart of the Diaphragm Shu. Used for diaphragmatic disorders: hiccups, vomiting, difficulty swallowing.
memoryTrick: Ge Guan = 'Diaphragm Pass' — the gateway (Guan) of the diaphragm (Ge) at T7, paired with Ge Shu on the inner line.
cautions: Oblique insertion to avoid pneumothorax.

name: Hun Men
location: 3 cun lateral to the lower border of the spinous process of the 9th thoracic vertebra (T9).
boardPearl: At T9 level (same as UB-18 Gan Shu). 'Gate of the Hun (Ethereal Soul)' — the Hun resides in the Liver. Used for Liver-spirit disorders: depression, frustration, lack of direction, and dream-disturbed sleep. The outer line addresses the spirit aspect of the Liver.
memoryTrick: Hun Men = 'Gate of the Hun' — the Ethereal Soul (Hun) lives in the Liver, and this is its gate at the Liver level (T9). 'The Hun wanders through its gate at night (dreaming).'
cautions: Oblique insertion to avoid pneumothorax.

name: Yang Gang
location: 3 cun lateral to the lower border of the spinous process of the 10th thoracic vertebra (T10).
boardPearl: At T10 level (same as UB-19 Dan Shu). 'Yang Headrope' — the Gallbladder is a Yang organ that governs decision-making. Used for Gallbladder-related digestive issues: jaundice, bitter taste, diarrhea.
memoryTrick: Yang Gang = 'Yang Headrope' — the Gallbladder (Yang) holds the headrope (leadership) of decision-making. At T10 = Gallbladder level.
cautions: Oblique insertion to avoid pneumothorax.

name: Yi She
location: 3 cun lateral to the lower border of the spinous process of the 11th thoracic vertebra (T11).
boardPearl: At T11 level (same as UB-20 Pi Shu). 'Abode of Thought' — the Yi (Thought/Intellect) resides in the Spleen. Used for Spleen-spirit disorders: overthinking, poor concentration, obsessive thoughts, and digestive issues from worry.
memoryTrick: Yi She = 'Abode of Thought' — the Yi (intellect) lives in the Spleen. This is its abode at the Spleen level (T11). 'Thinking too much? Visit the Yi's home at T11.'
cautions: Oblique insertion.

name: Wei Cang
location: 3 cun lateral to the lower border of the spinous process of the 12th thoracic vertebra (T12).
boardPearl: At T12 level (same as UB-21 Wei Shu). 'Stomach Granary' — the storehouse of the Stomach. Used for Stomach-related digestive disorders. The outer line counterpart of the Stomach Shu.
memoryTrick: Wei Cang = 'Stomach Granary' — the Stomach's storehouse (Cang) at T12, matching Wei Shu on the inner line.
cautions: Oblique insertion.

name: Huang Men
location: 3 cun lateral to the lower border of the spinous process of the 1st lumbar vertebra (L1).
boardPearl: At L1 level (same as UB-22 San Jiao Shu). 'Vitals Gate' — the gate of the Huang (vital membranes). Used for abdominal masses and breast lumps.
memoryTrick: Huang Men = 'Vitals Gate' — the gate to the vital membranes at L1, the outer counterpart of San Jiao Shu.

name: Zhi Shi
location: 3 cun lateral to the lower border of the spinous process of the 2nd lumbar vertebra (L2).
boardPearl: At L2 level (same as UB-23 Shen Shu). 'Will Chamber' — the Zhi (Will/Willpower) resides in the Kidney. Used for Kidney-spirit disorders: lack of willpower, fear, and also for sexual/reproductive issues. The outer line counterpart of Kidney Shu addresses the spirit aspect of the Kidneys.
memoryTrick: Zhi Shi = 'Will Chamber' — the Zhi (willpower) lives in the Kidney. This is the Will's room at the Kidney level (L2). 'Your willpower is stored in the Kidney's chamber.'

name: Bao Huang
location: 3 cun lateral to the midline, at the level of the 2nd posterior sacral foramen (S2).
boardPearl: At S2 level (same as UB-28 Pang Guang Shu), on the outer Bladder line. 'Bladder Vitals' — treats the vital region of the Bladder organ. Used for urinary and sacral disorders.
memoryTrick: Bao Huang = 'Bladder Vitals' — wrapping (Bao) the vital membranes (Huang) of the Bladder at S2 on the outer line.

name: Zhi Bian
location: 3 cun lateral to the midline, at the level of the 4th posterior sacral foramen (S4), at the level of the lower border of the sacrum.
indications: muscular atrophy of the lower limbs
boardPearl: Last point of the second (outer) Bladder line before the two lines merge. Critical point for SCIATICA — deep needling here reaches the piriformis and sciatic nerve area. Also important for hemorrhoids and sacral/gluteal pain. At S4 level, 3 cun lateral.
memoryTrick: Zhi Bian = 'Sequential Limit' — the outer Bladder line reaches its limit (end) here. Think: 'the Limit of sciatica treatment' — a go-to point for sciatic pain.
cautions: Deep needling may reach the sciatic nerve — use caution with depth and angle.

name: He Yang
location: 2 cun directly below UB-40 (Wei Zhong), between the medial and lateral heads of the gastrocnemius muscle.
boardPearl: Located 2 cun below UB-40 on the posterior calf. 'Yang Union' — where the two branches of the Bladder channel unite after the popliteal fossa. Treats lower back pain and posterior leg pain.
memoryTrick: He Yang = 'Yang Union' — the two Yang Bladder lines unite (He = unite) below the knee at this point.

name: Cheng Jin
location: 5 cun below UB-40, at the center of the belly of the gastrocnemius muscle, between UB-55 and UB-57.
indications: Spasm and pain of the calf
boardPearl: Located at the belly of the gastrocnemius, 5 cun below UB-40. 'Sinew Support' — supports the sinews/tendons of the calf. Used for calf cramps and spasms.
memoryTrick: Cheng Jin = 'Sinew Support' — supports (Cheng) the sinew (Jin) of the calf muscle.

name: Cheng Shan
location: On the posterior aspect of the lower leg, 8 cun below UB-40, in the depression formed below the bellies of the gastrocnemius muscle when the muscle is flexed, at the junction of the two muscle bellies.
boardPearl: THE premier point for HEMORRHOIDS. Also excellent for calf cramps, spasms, and posterior leg pain. Located at the inverted V-shape where the two heads of the gastrocnemius meet. 'Mountain Support' — the calf looks like a mountain, and this point supports it.
memoryTrick: Cheng Shan = 'Mountain Support' — the calf muscle forms a 'mountain' shape, and this point is at the peak. Think: 'Mountain of hemorrhoids' — the #1 hemorrhoid point.

name: Fei Yang
location: 7 cun directly above UB-60 (Kun Lun), 1 cun inferior and lateral to UB-57, on the posterior border of the fibula.
boardPearl: Luo-Connecting point of the Urinary Bladder channel, connecting to KD (Kidney). As a Luo point, it treats both the Bladder and Kidney channels. Used for chronic headache, nasal obstruction, low back pain, and leg disorders. Luo points treat both interior and exterior paired channels.
memoryTrick: Fei Yang = 'Taking Flight' — the Luo vessel takes flight (Fei Yang) from the Bladder to connect to the Kidney channel. Like a bird flying from one channel to the other.

name: Fu Yang
location: 3 cun directly above UB-60 (Kun Lun), on the posterior border of the fibula.
categories: Xi-Cleft point of Yang Qiao Mai
indications: redness and swelling of the lateral malleolus
boardPearl: Xi-Cleft point of the YANG QIAO MAI (not the Bladder channel itself). Xi-Cleft points treat acute conditions of their associated channel — so for acute Yang Qiao Mai disorders. Located 3 cun above UB-60.
memoryTrick: Fu Yang = 'Instep Yang' — Yang at the instep/ankle area. Remember it as the Xi-Cleft of Yang Qiao Mai: 'Fu Yang guards the Yang stepping vessel.'
specialCategories: Xi-Cleft point of Yang Qiao Mai

name: Kun Lun
location: In the depression between the tip of the lateral malleolus and the Achilles tendon.
categories: Ma Dan Yang Heavenly Star point
indications: swelling and pain of the ankle
boardPearl: Jing-River/FIRE point of the Bladder channel. Ma Dan Yang Heavenly Star point. CONTRAINDICATED IN PREGNANCY (can induce labor). Major point for low back pain, sciatica, and occipital headache. Used to promote labor and for difficult delivery. 'The great mountain between the two peaks' — lateral malleolus and Achilles tendon.
memoryTrick: Kun Lun = 'Kunlun Mountains' — the lateral malleolus is like a great mountain peak. Fire point of Water channel: 'Fire on the mountain.' PREGNANCY WARNING: 'Don't climb mountains when pregnant!'
cautions: CONTRAINDICATED in pregnancy — strongly promotes labor. Can cause premature labor.
specialCategories: Ma Dan Yang Heavenly Star point

name: Pu Can
location: On the lateral aspect of the foot, directly below the lateral malleolus, 1.5 cun below UB-60, in the depression at the junction of the red and white skin.
indications: swelling and pain of the foot
muscular atrophy of the lower limbs
boardPearl: Located below the lateral malleolus at the heel area. 'Subservient Visitor' — used for heel pain and foot disorders. A minor point, less commonly tested.
memoryTrick: Pu Can = 'Subservient Visitor' — like a servant kneeling (on the heel) to visit. Located at the heel where a servant would kneel.

name: Shen Mai
location: In the depression directly below the tip of the lateral malleolus.
categories: Confluent point of the Yang Qiao Mai
boardPearl: CONFLUENT (Opening) point of the YANG QIAO MAI, paired with SI-3 (Du Mai) in the Eight Extraordinary Vessel system. Together they treat the Du Mai, Yang Qiao Mai, inner canthus, ears, shoulders, back, and lower limbs. Important for insomnia (excess type — Yang Qiao Mai excess keeps you awake), epilepsy, and lateral ankle disorders.
memoryTrick: Shen Mai = 'Extending Vessel' — it extends/opens the Yang Qiao Mai (Yang Stepping Vessel). Paired with SI-3: 'Shen Mai steps (Qiao) while SI-3 governs (Du).' For INSOMNIA: Yang Qiao excess = too much Yang at night = can't sleep.
specialCategories: Confluent point of Yang Qiao Mai

name: Jin Men
location: On the lateral aspect of the foot, posterior and inferior to the tuberosity of the 5th metatarsal bone, at the junction of the red and white skin.
categories: Xi-Cleft point of the Bladder channel
indications: pain of the lateral aspect of the lower limb
boardPearl: Xi-Cleft point of the BLADDER channel. Xi-Cleft points treat ACUTE conditions — so UB-63 is for acute Bladder channel pain (acute back pain, acute urinary conditions). Important for epilepsy and infantile convulsions. Do not confuse with UB-59 (Xi-Cleft of Yang Qiao Mai).
memoryTrick: Jin Men = 'Metal Gate' — the golden (Jin) gate that clefts open for acute emergencies. Xi-Cleft of UB: 'Jin Men is the emergency gate of the Bladder.'
specialCategories: Xi-Cleft point of the Bladder channel

name: Jing Gu
location: On the lateral aspect of the foot, posterior and inferior to the tuberosity of the 5th metatarsal bone, at the junction of the red and white skin.
indications: pain of the lower back and thigh
boardPearl: Yuan-Source point of the Bladder channel. Yuan-Source points access the original Qi of the organ — UB-64 accesses the Bladder's Yuan Qi. Often combined with the Luo point of the paired channel (KD-4) for treatment. Named 'Capital Bone' for the prominent 5th metatarsal tuberosity.
memoryTrick: Jing Gu = 'Capital Bone' — the big (capital) bone of the 5th metatarsal. Yuan-Source point: 'The capital city is the source of the nation, just as Jing Gu is the source of the Bladder.'

name: Shu Gu
location: On the lateral aspect of the foot, posterior and inferior to the head of the 5th metatarsal bone, at the junction of the red and white skin.
boardPearl: Shu-Stream/WOOD point and the SEDATION point of the Bladder channel. Sedation point because Wood is the child of Water (Water generates Wood — drain the child to sedate the mother). Used to reduce excess in the Bladder channel. Shu-Stream points treat heaviness and joint pain.
memoryTrick: Shu Gu = 'Bundle Bone' — bones bundled together at the 5th metatarsal head. SEDATION point: 'Wood is Water's child — sedate the child (Wood/UB-65) to calm the mother (Water/UB).'

name: Zu Tong Gu
location: On the lateral aspect of the foot, in the depression anterior and inferior to the 5th metatarsophalangeal joint, at the junction of the red and white skin.
boardPearl: Ying-Spring/WATER point of the Bladder channel. Ying-Spring points clear heat from the channel — this one clears Heat from the Bladder channel. Water point on a Water channel — this is the horary point (same element as channel). Used for headache and epistaxis from Bladder channel heat.
memoryTrick: Zu Tong Gu = 'Foot Valley Passage' — passage through the valley of the foot. Water point on Water channel = the HORARY point. 'Double Water in the valley.'

name: Zhi Yin
location: On the lateral side of the little toe, 0.1 cun proximal to the corner of the nail.
depth: 0.1 cun perpendicular, or prick to bleed. Moxa is the primary technique for turning breech presentation.
boardPearl: Jing-Well/METAL point and TONIFICATION point of the Bladder channel. Tonification because Metal is the mother of Water (Metal generates Water — tonify the mother to strengthen the child). FAMOUS for turning BREECH BABIES with moxa — apply moxa to UB-67 bilaterally. Also used to promote labor and for retained placenta. The last point on the channel, connecting to KD-1 on the sole.
memoryTrick: Zhi Yin = 'Reaching Yin' — the Yang Bladder channel reaches the Yin (Kidney channel) at the little toe, transitioning to KD-1 on the sole. BREECH BABY: 'Burn (moxa) the Yin to turn the baby.' Metal mother tonifies Water child.
cautions: Use moxa for breech baby — typically applied bilaterally for 15-20 minutes daily. Contraindicated for strong stimulation in pregnancy except when specifically used for breech presentation.

name: Hui Yin
location: On the perineum, midway between the anus and the root of the scrotum in males, or midway between the anus and the posterior labial commissure in females
categories: Meeting point of Ren Mai, Du Mai, and Chong Mai
boardPearl: Meeting point of the three extraordinary vessels: Ren Mai, Du Mai, and Chong Mai. The Ren and Du start here — the circuit of Yin and Yang begins at the perineum. One of Sun Si Miao's Ghost points (Gui Cang) for mania and psychological disorders.
memoryTrick: Meeting of Yin = where all the Yin meets. Three extraordinary vessels (Ren, Du, Chong) converge at the lowest Yin point of the body — the perineum is the foundation.
cautions: Rarely needled in clinical practice due to sensitivity of the location. Often moxa is used instead. Contraindicated during pregnancy.

name: Qu Gu
location: On the anterior midline, at the superior border of the pubic symphysis
categories: Meeting point of Ren Mai and Liver channel
boardPearl: Meeting point of Ren Mai and the Liver channel. Located at the superior border of the pubic symphysis — the 'curved bone' refers to the pubic bone. Important for urogenital disorders.
memoryTrick: Curved Bone = the pubic bone (os pubis) curves. Right on top of the pubic symphysis — the anatomical landmark is the bone itself.
cautions: Needle with caution when bladder is full. Contraindicated during pregnancy.

name: Zhong Ji
location: On the anterior midline, 4 cun below the umbilicus, 1 cun above the pubic symphysis
categories: Meeting point of Ren Mai with SP, LV, and KD channels
boardPearl: Front-Mu of the Bladder. Meeting point of Ren Mai with three Yin leg channels (SP, LV, KD). Key point for ALL urogenital disorders. As the Mu point of the Bladder, it diagnoses and treats Bladder pathology — especially urinary retention and incontinence.
memoryTrick: Central Pole = the central axis pole at 4 cun below the navel. It's the Bladder's Mu point — think 'central pole holds the Bladder.' Three Yin channels of the leg meet here (SP, LV, KD).
cautions: Contraindicated during pregnancy. Ensure bladder is empty before needling.

name: Guan Yuan
location: On the anterior midline, 3 cun below the umbilicus
categories: Meeting point of Ren Mai with SP, LV, and KD channels
boardPearl: Front-Mu of the Small Intestine. Meeting of Ren Mai with SP, LV, KD. One of the most important points for tonifying Qi, Blood, Yin, and Yang — especially with moxa. Strengthens Kidney Qi and Yuan (Source) Qi. The name 'Gate of Origin' refers to the source of Original Qi (Yuan Qi). With moxa, it rescues Yang collapse.
memoryTrick: Gate of Origin = the gate to your Original (Yuan) Qi. 3 cun below navel. SI Mu point at the gate where original Qi is stored. Think: 'Guan Yuan guards the origin of life.'
cautions: Contraindicated during pregnancy. Use moxa for tonification; needling with reducing method may disperse Yuan Qi.

name: Shi Men
location: On the anterior midline, 2 cun below the umbilicus
categories: Front-Mu of the San Jiao (Triple Burner)
boardPearl: Front-Mu of the San Jiao. CRITICAL BOARD POINT: Traditionally contraindicated in women wishing to conceive — classical texts state it causes infertility if needled. The name 'Stone Gate' implies blocking (making the womb like stone). This is a high-yield test question.
memoryTrick: Stone Gate = the gate turns the uterus to stone (infertility). NEVER needle in women trying to conceive. San Jiao Mu point — the SJ manages water passages, and this is 2 cun below the navel.
cautions: MAJOR CAUTION: Traditionally contraindicated for women desiring pregnancy. Classical texts (e.g., Zhen Jiu Jia Yi Jing) warn that needling this point causes infertility. Contraindicated during pregnancy.

name: Qi Hai
location: On the anterior midline, 1.5 cun below the umbilicus
boardPearl: One of the most important points for tonifying Qi in the entire body. The 'Sea of Qi' is where Qi gathers and is generated. Combined with REN-4, it is the primary prescription for Qi and Yang deficiency. Moxa here strongly tonifies and warms Yang Qi. Often combined with ST-36 for severe Qi deficiency.
memoryTrick: Sea of Qi = the ocean of Qi, 1.5 cun below the navel. If a patient is exhausted and Qi-deficient, think Qi Hai. The sea is vast — it tonifies ALL Qi.
cautions: Contraindicated during pregnancy.

name: Yin Jiao
location: On the anterior midline, 1 cun below the umbilicus
categories: Meeting point of Ren Mai, Kidney channel, and Chong Mai
boardPearl: Meeting point of Ren Mai, KD channel, and Chong Mai. The 'Yin Intersection' is where these Yin vessels cross. Treats gynecological disorders due to its connection with the Chong Mai (Sea of Blood).
memoryTrick: Yin Intersection = where the Yin vessels (Ren, KD, Chong) intersect, 1 cun below the navel. Don't confuse with DU-28 (also named Yin Jiao).
cautions: Contraindicated during pregnancy.

name: Shen Que
location: In the center of the umbilicus
depth: NO NEEDLING — moxa only (indirect moxa with salt or ginger)
boardPearl: ABSOLUTELY NO NEEDLING — moxa only. This is a critical board point. Indirect moxa (typically with salt filling the navel, then moxa on top) is used to rescue Yang collapse and warm the interior. The navel is the former connection to prenatal Qi via the umbilical cord — hence 'Spirit Palace Gate.'
memoryTrick: Spirit Palace Gate = the navel was the spirit's gate to prenatal life (umbilical cord). NEVER NEEDLE the navel. Only moxa — typically salt moxa for emergencies (Yang collapse/diarrhea).
cautions: ABSOLUTELY CONTRAINDICATED FOR NEEDLING. Moxa only — use indirect moxa with salt or ginger. Risk of peritonitis if needled.

name: Shui Fen
location: On the anterior midline, 1 cun above the umbilicus
boardPearl: Water Divide — the point where water (fluids) are separated in the body. This is where the Small Intestine separates the pure from the turbid. Key point for edema and fluid accumulation, especially abdominal ascites.
memoryTrick: Water Divide = water is divided (separated) here, 1 cun above the navel. The SI separates pure fluids from turbid — this point regulates that function. Think: water problems above the navel? Shui Fen.
cautions: Contraindicated during pregnancy.

name: Xia Wan
location: On the anterior midline, 2 cun above the umbilicus
categories: Meeting point of Ren Mai and Spleen channel
boardPearl: Lower Epigastrium — the lower part of the Stomach. Meeting point of Ren Mai and the Spleen channel. Part of the three Wan points (REN-10 Xia Wan, REN-12 Zhong Wan, REN-13 Shang Wan) that treat the Stomach. Xia Wan is best for food stagnation and disorders of the lower part of the Stomach.
memoryTrick: Xia (Lower) Wan (Epigastrium) = lower stomach region, 2 cun above the navel. The three Wan points: Xia (lower, REN-10), Zhong (middle, REN-12), Shang (upper, REN-13).
cautions: Contraindicated during pregnancy.

name: Jian Li
location: On the anterior midline, 3 cun above the umbilicus
boardPearl: Located midway between the umbilicus and REN-12. The name 'Interior Strengthening' suggests its role in strengthening the middle Jiao. Less commonly tested than the three Wan points.
memoryTrick: Interior Strengthening = strengthens the interior (middle Jiao), 3 cun above the navel. Halfway between the navel and REN-12.
cautions: Contraindicated during pregnancy.

name: Zhong Wan
location: On the anterior midline, 4 cun above the umbilicus, midway between the umbilicus and the sternocostal angle
categories: Hui-Influential point of the Fu organs
Meeting point of Ren Mai with ST, SI, and SJ channels
boardPearl: EXTREMELY HIGH-YIELD. Front-Mu of the Stomach AND Hui-Influential point of all six Fu organs. Meeting of Ren with ST, SI, and SJ. As the Influential point of Fu organs, it treats ALL hollow organ disorders. Combined with ST-36 (He-Sea of ST), it is the most important prescription for digestive disorders. As a Mu point, it diagnoses and treats Stomach pathology.
memoryTrick: Zhong (Central/Middle) Wan (Epigastrium) = the middle of the stomach, 4 cun above navel. It's the MOST IMPORTANT digestive point — Mu of Stomach + Influential of ALL Fu organs. Think: 'Central command for all hollow organs.'
cautions: Contraindicated during pregnancy.

name: Shang Wan
location: On the anterior midline, 5 cun above the umbilicus
categories: Meeting point of Ren Mai with ST and SI channels
boardPearl: Upper Epigastrium — the upper part of the Stomach. Meeting point of Ren Mai with ST and SI channels. Best for disorders of the upper Stomach (cardia), especially rebellious Stomach Qi (vomiting, hiccup, nausea). Part of the three Wan points.
memoryTrick: Shang (Upper) Wan (Epigastrium) = upper stomach region, 5 cun above the navel. The three Wans: Shang (upper, 5 cun), Zhong (middle, 4 cun), Xia (lower, 2 cun).
cautions: Contraindicated during pregnancy.

name: Ju Que
location: On the anterior midline, 6 cun above the umbilicus, 2 cun below the sternocostal angle
boardPearl: Front-Mu of the Heart. The 'Great Palace' is where the Heart (Emperor) resides. As the Heart's Mu point, it diagnoses and treats Heart pathology — especially chest pain, palpitations, and mental/emotional disorders. Important that the Heart Mu is on Ren Mai (not on the Heart channel itself).
memoryTrick: Great Palace = the emperor's (Heart's) grand palace. The Heart's Front-Mu is at 6 cun above the navel. Think: 'The great palace of the Heart is on the Ren channel, not the Heart channel.'
cautions: Do not needle deeply — risk of penetrating the peritoneum. Contraindicated during pregnancy.

name: Jiu Wei
location: On the anterior midline, 1 cun below the sternocostal angle (xiphoid process), 7 cun above the umbilicus
categories: Luo-Connecting point of the Ren Mai
boardPearl: Luo-Connecting point of Ren Mai. The Luo point connects Ren Mai to the whole abdomen and chest. The xiphoid process resembles a turtledove's tail. As the Luo point, it treats conditions of the Ren Mai and its associated organs. Important for chest and epigastric pain, and calming the spirit.
memoryTrick: Turtledove Tail = the xiphoid process looks like a dove's tail. The Luo point of Ren Mai is just below the xiphoid — the 'tail' of the sternum. Luo-Connecting points spread Qi widely.
cautions: Do not needle deeply — the xiphoid is superficial and there is risk of peritoneal penetration. Contraindicated during pregnancy.

name: Zhong Ting
location: On the anterior midline, at the level of the 5th intercostal space, on the sternum, midway between REN-15 and REN-17
boardPearl: Located on the sternum between REN-15 and REN-17. Less commonly tested. Treats chest fullness and difficulty swallowing. The name 'Central Palace' indicates it is in the center of the chest palace.
memoryTrick: Central Palace = in the center of the chest, between the turtledove tail (REN-15) and the chest center (REN-17). A minor point — know its location between two major points.
cautions: Needle subcutaneously only — sternum is superficial.

name: Dan Zhong
location: On the anterior midline, at the level of the 4th intercostal space, midway between the nipples (in males), on the sternum
categories: Meeting point of Ren Mai with SP, KD, SI, and SJ channels
boardPearl: EXTREMELY HIGH-YIELD. Front-Mu of the Pericardium AND Hui-Influential point of Qi AND Sea of Qi. Also called Shan Zhong. The most important point for Qi in the chest. As the Influential point of Qi, it treats ALL Qi disorders — Qi stagnation, Qi deficiency, rebellious Qi. Combined with REN-6 (Sea of Qi below), these two points regulate Qi throughout the body. Also used for insufficient lactation (breast milk is a transformation of Qi and Blood).
memoryTrick: Dan Zhong (Chest Center) = center of the chest between the nipples. Triple-category point: Mu of PC + Influential of Qi + Sea of Qi. Think: 'The chest center commands all Qi.' Also known as Shan Zhong.
cautions: Needle subcutaneously only — sternum is superficial. Contraindicated during pregnancy.

name: Yu Tang
location: On the anterior midline, at the level of the 3rd intercostal space, on the sternum
boardPearl: Located on the sternum at the 3rd intercostal space. 'Jade Hall' poetically refers to the area housing the Heart and Lungs. Less commonly tested individually.
memoryTrick: Jade Hall = a precious hall on the sternum above the chest center. The Heart and Lungs reside in this jade hall.
cautions: Needle subcutaneously only — sternum is superficial.

name: Zi Gong
location: On the anterior midline, at the level of the 2nd intercostal space, on the sternum
boardPearl: Located on the sternum at the 2nd intercostal space. 'Purple Palace' is another name for the residence of the Emperor (Heart). Not commonly tested. Do NOT confuse with the extra point Zi Gong (uterus point).
memoryTrick: Purple Palace = the imperial (purple) palace of the Heart. Located on the sternum. Don't confuse with the extra point Zi Gong near REN-3.
cautions: Needle subcutaneously only — sternum is superficial.

name: Hua Gai
location: On the anterior midline, at the level of the 1st intercostal space, on the sternum (manubriosternal joint/angle of Louis)
boardPearl: Located at the sternal angle (angle of Louis). 'Florid Canopy' refers to a ceremonial umbrella — the Lung is the canopy that covers and protects the organs below. Treats Lung Qi disorders.
memoryTrick: Florid Canopy = the Lung as a canopy over the organs. Located at the sternal angle. The canopy is the highest point of the chest (Lung).
cautions: Needle subcutaneously only.

name: Xuan Ji
location: On the anterior midline, on the manubrium, 1 cun below REN-22, at the center of the manubrium
boardPearl: Located on the manubrium of the sternum. 'Jade Pivot' — a celestial reference, the pivot between the throat and chest. Less commonly tested.
memoryTrick: Jade Pivot = the pivot point on the manubrium, between REN-22 (throat) and the chest points below. The jade pivot connects throat to chest.
cautions: Needle subcutaneously only.

name: Tian Tu
location: On the anterior midline, in the center of the suprasternal fossa, above the sternal notch
depth: First 0.2 cun perpendicularly, then direct the needle inferiorly along the posterior aspect of the sternum for 0.5-1 cun
boardPearl: Window of Heaven point. Located in the suprasternal fossa — the 'celestial chimney' through which Qi ascends to the head. UNIQUE NEEDLING TECHNIQUE: needle first perpendicularly, then redirect inferiorly behind the sternum. Critical for all throat and voice disorders. Important for Plum-pit Qi (sensation of a lump in the throat).
memoryTrick: Celestial Chimney = the chimney (trachea) opens to the heavens (head) at the throat. Window of Heaven = regulates Qi between head and body. Special needling: perpendicular first, then angle DOWN behind the sternum.
cautions: CRITICAL NEEDLING CAUTION: Must angle the needle inferiorly behind the sternum after initial perpendicular insertion. Never needle deeply in a perpendicular direction — risk of damaging trachea, esophagus, and major vessels. Do not needle superiorly.

name: Lian Quan
location: On the anterior midline, in the depression above the hyoid bone, below the chin
depth: 0.5-1 cun obliquely superiorly toward the root of the tongue
indications: Swelling and pain below the tongue
boardPearl: Located above the hyoid bone. The 'Ridge Spring' is at the ridge of the throat where saliva springs forth. Key point for tongue and speech disorders — aphasia after stroke, tongue stiffness, drooling. Needle obliquely toward the root of the tongue.
memoryTrick: Ridge Spring = the ridge (hyoid bone) where the spring (saliva) flows. Think tongue and speech problems — the spring under the tongue. Needle toward the tongue root.
cautions: Needle carefully — avoid the major vessels of the neck.

name: Cheng Jiang
location: On the anterior midline, in the depression in the center of the mentolabial groove (below the lower lip)
categories: Meeting point of Ren Mai, Du Mai, ST, and LI channels
boardPearl: Meeting point of Ren Mai with Du Mai, ST, and LI channels. The terminal point of Ren Mai where it connects with Du Mai — completing the Ren-Du circuit. One of Sun Si Miao's Ghost points (Gui Shi). 'Sauce Receptacle' = the depression below the lip catches sauce/drool. Important for facial paralysis and deviation of the mouth.
memoryTrick: Sauce Receptacle = the dip below the lip catches sauce (saliva). This is where Ren meets Du (and ST/LI) — the Yin-Yang circuit connects at the face. Ghost point for mental disorders.

name: Chang Qiang
location: Below the tip of the coccyx, midway between the coccyx and the anus, located in the prone position
depth: 0.5-1 cun obliquely superiorly along the anterior surface of the coccyx
categories: Meeting point of Du Mai, Ren Mai, and Kidney channel
indications: Pain in the lower back and coccyx
boardPearl: Luo-Connecting point of Du Mai. Meeting of Du, Ren, and KD. The Luo point connects Du Mai to Ren Mai, linking the Yang sea to the Yin sea. 'Long Strong' refers to the strength of the spine (the 'long' backbone). Primary point for hemorrhoids and rectal prolapse — it is the local point at the coccyx/anus.
memoryTrick: Long Strong = the long spine starts strong here at the coccyx. Du Mai's Luo connects to Ren Mai. Think: 'The long strong backbone begins at the tailbone, treating hemorrhoids on the way.'
cautions: Needle obliquely superiorly. Do not puncture the rectum.

name: Yao Shu
location: On the posterior midline, at the sacral hiatus
indications: Pain and stiffness of the lower back
boardPearl: Located at the sacral hiatus (the opening at the bottom of the sacral canal). 'Lumbar Shu' refers to the transport point of the lumbar region. Treats lower back pain and lower limb disorders via its location on the sacrum.
memoryTrick: Lumbar Shu = the Shu (transport) point at the lumbar/sacral region. The sacral hiatus is the 'doorway' into the sacral canal.

name: Yao Yang Guan
location: On the posterior midline, in the depression below the spinous process of the 4th lumbar vertebra (L4)
indications: Pain and stiffness of the lower back
boardPearl: Located below L4 spinous process. The 'Yang Gate' of the lumbar region — Yang Qi must pass through this gate to reach the lower body and lower extremities. Important for lower back pain and weakness/numbness of the legs. Also treats reproductive disorders because Yang Qi must flow through here to warm the lower Jiao.
memoryTrick: Lumbar Yang Gate = the gate through which Yang Qi passes to the legs. At L4 — same level as the iliac crest landmark. If Yang can't pass the gate, legs are weak and cold.

name: Ming Men
location: On the posterior midline, in the depression below the spinous process of the 2nd lumbar vertebra (L2)
indications: Stiffness and pain of the lower back
boardPearl: EXTREMELY HIGH-YIELD. 'Gate of Vitality' — also called the 'Gate of Life.' Located between L2 spinous processes, directly posterior to REN-4 (Guan Yuan). Houses the Ming Men Fire (Kidney Yang/Ministerial Fire). Moxa here strongly tonifies Kidney Yang. Essential for all Kidney Yang deficiency patterns — cold limbs, impotence, infertility, chronic diarrhea. Directly between the two Kidneys.
memoryTrick: Ming Men = Gate of Life, between L2 spinous processes. Directly behind REN-4. The fire of life (Kidney Yang) resides here. Think: 'Ming Men fire warms the entire body — moxa the gate of life for Yang deficiency.'

name: Xuan Shu
location: On the posterior midline, in the depression below the spinous process of the 1st lumbar vertebra (L1)
indications: Stiffness and pain of the lower back
boardPearl: Located below L1 spinous process. 'Suspended Pivot' — the pivot of the lower back. Treats lower back pain and digestive disorders. Less commonly tested than DU-4.
memoryTrick: Suspended Pivot = the pivot point of the lower back at L1. The spine pivots and bends here — suspended between upper and lower body.

name: Ji Zhong
location: On the posterior midline, in the depression below the spinous process of the 11th thoracic vertebra (T11)
indications: Stiffness and pain of the back
boardPearl: Located below T11 spinous process. 'Spine Center' — the center of the spinal column. Treats diarrhea, jaundice, and epilepsy. At the same level as the Spleen Back-Shu (BL-20).
memoryTrick: Spine Center = the center of the spine at T11. Near the Spleen Back-Shu level — hence treats diarrhea and jaundice (Spleen/Liver territory).

name: Zhong Shu
location: On the posterior midline, in the depression below the spinous process of the 10th thoracic vertebra (T10)
indications: Stiffness and pain of the back
boardPearl: Located below T10 spinous process. 'Central Pivot' — another pivot point on the spine. Less commonly tested.
memoryTrick: Central Pivot = the central pivot of the back at T10. Between DU-6 (T11) and DU-8 (T9).

name: Jin Suo
location: On the posterior midline, in the depression below the spinous process of the 9th thoracic vertebra (T9)
indications: Stiffness and pain of the back
boardPearl: Located below T9 spinous process. 'Sinew Contraction' — treats contraction and spasm of the sinews (muscles). Key point for muscle spasm, stiffness of the back, and epilepsy (where the body contracts in seizures). At the level of the Liver Back-Shu (BL-18) — the Liver governs the sinews.
memoryTrick: Sinew Contraction = sinews (muscles) contract and spasm. At T9 level near the Liver Back-Shu — the Liver governs sinews. Treats epilepsy (body contracting in seizures) and muscle spasms.

name: Zhi Yang
location: On the posterior midline, in the depression below the spinous process of the 7th thoracic vertebra (T7), approximately at the level of the inferior angle of the scapula
indications: Stiffness and pain of the back
boardPearl: Located below T7 spinous process — the landmark is the inferior angle of the scapula. 'Reaching Yang' — Yang Qi reaches its fullness here. Important for jaundice (this level corresponds to the Diaphragm Back-Shu BL-17, and the area between the Liver and Gallbladder Back-Shu points). Also known as the point where the diaphragm separates upper and lower body.
memoryTrick: Reaching Yang = Yang Qi reaches here at T7. LANDMARK: inferior angle of the scapula = T7. Think: 'Reaching Yang at the scapula's bottom tip.' Key for jaundice at the diaphragm level.

name: Ling Tai
location: On the posterior midline, in the depression below the spinous process of the 6th thoracic vertebra (T6)
boardPearl: Located below T6 spinous process. 'Spirit Tower' — the tower of the spirit near the Heart. Sometimes used for boils and skin lesions on the back. Less commonly tested.
memoryTrick: Spirit Tower = a tower for the spirit at T6. The tower stands near the Heart region of the back.

name: Shen Dao
location: On the posterior midline, in the depression below the spinous process of the 5th thoracic vertebra (T5)
boardPearl: Located below T5 spinous process — at the level of the Heart Back-Shu (BL-15). 'Spirit Path' — the pathway of the Shen (spirit). The Heart houses the Shen, and this point is directly on the Du Mai at the Heart level. Treats Heart spirit disorders: anxiety, palpitations, poor memory, insomnia.
memoryTrick: Spirit Path = the path of the Shen (Heart spirit) at T5. At the same level as BL-15 (Heart Back-Shu). Think: 'The spirit walks this path at the Heart level.'

name: Shen Zhu
location: On the posterior midline, in the depression below the spinous process of the 3rd thoracic vertebra (T3)
indications: Stiffness and pain of the back
boardPearl: Located below T3 spinous process — at the level of the Lung Back-Shu (BL-13). 'Body Pillar' — the pillar that supports the body. Classically, one of the most important points for PEDIATRIC conditions, especially childhood epilepsy, asthma, and failure to thrive. Moxa at DU-12 is a traditional pediatric treatment.
memoryTrick: Body Pillar = the pillar of the body's spine at T3. Think PEDIATRICS — the body pillar supports growing children. Key for childhood epilepsy and developmental issues.
cautions: Use care with moxa in children — gentle techniques recommended.

name: Tao Dao
location: On the posterior midline, in the depression below the spinous process of the 1st thoracic vertebra (T1)
categories: Meeting point of Du Mai and Bladder channel
indications: Stiffness of the back and neck
boardPearl: Located below T1 spinous process. Meeting point of Du Mai and the Bladder channel. 'Kiln Path' — the kiln (furnace) generates heat, and this point treats febrile diseases and malaria. Used to release the exterior and clear heat from the Du Mai.
memoryTrick: Kiln Path = the path through a kiln (heat). Treats febrile diseases at T1. Meeting of Du and BL — the Bladder channel (Taiyang) governs the exterior, and Du governs all Yang.

name: Da Zhui
location: On the posterior midline, in the depression below the spinous process of the 7th cervical vertebra (C7), approximately at the level of the shoulders
categories: Meeting point of Du Mai with ALL six Yang channels (LI, SI, SJ, ST, BL, GB)
boardPearl: EXTREMELY HIGH-YIELD — one of the most tested points. Meeting point of Du Mai with ALL SIX Yang channels. The 'Great Hammer' hammers out all heat and fever. This is THE point for clearing heat from the body, whether excess or deficiency heat. C7 (vertebra prominens) is the most prominent spinous process at the base of the neck — the anatomical landmark. Used for any febrile disease, malaria, and to tonify Yang (with moxa). Commonly bled with a three-edged needle for high fever.
memoryTrick: Great Hammer = the great hammer at C7 hammers all Yang channels (all 6 meet here). The most prominent vertebra = C7. Think: 'Da Zhui is the grand meeting hall of all Yang — hammer down any fever.' High-yield for boards: meeting of ALL Yang + Du.

name: Ya Men
location: On the posterior midline, in the depression 0.5 cun below DU-16, at the nape of the neck, 0.5 cun above the posterior hairline, in the depression between the 1st and 2nd cervical vertebrae (C1/C2)
depth: 0.5-1 cun perpendicularly — do NOT angle superiorly
categories: Meeting point of Du Mai and Yang Wei Mai
boardPearl: Window of Heaven point. Meeting of Du Mai and Yang Wei Mai. 'Mute's Gate' — the gate that controls speech. Key point for sudden loss of voice, aphasia after stroke, and speech disorders. Window of Heaven points regulate Qi between the head and body.
memoryTrick: Mute's Gate = the gate that, when blocked, causes muteness. Opens the gate to restore speech. Window of Heaven at the nape of the neck. Think: 'If the gate is closed, the person is mute — open Ya Men to restore the voice.'
cautions: CRITICAL CAUTION: Do NOT angle the needle superiorly — risk of entering the foramen magnum and puncturing the medulla oblongata. Needle strictly perpendicularly or slightly inferiorly. Do not exceed 1 cun depth. No moxa according to some classical texts.

name: Feng Fu
location: On the posterior midline, 1 cun above the posterior hairline, directly below the external occipital protuberance, in the depression between the trapezius muscles of both sides
depth: 0.5-1 cun perpendicularly — do NOT angle superiorly
categories: Meeting point of Du Mai, Yang Wei Mai, and Bladder channel
boardPearl: VERY HIGH-YIELD. Sea of Marrow point — connects to the Brain (Sea of Marrow). Meeting of Du Mai, Yang Wei Mai, and BL channel. Ghost point (Gui Zhen). Window of Heaven point. 'Wind Palace' — the palace where pathogenic Wind enters or lodges. This is the most important point for expelling Wind from the head and neck. Combined with GB-20 for Wind invasion.
memoryTrick: Wind Palace = wind (feng) enters and resides in this palace at the base of the skull. Sea of Marrow = connects to the Brain. Think: 'Wind attacks the palace and disturbs the sea of marrow (brain).' Below the external occipital protuberance.
cautions: CRITICAL CAUTION: Same as DU-15 — do NOT angle superiorly. Risk of entering the foramen magnum. Needle strictly perpendicularly. Do not exceed 1 cun depth.

name: Nao Hu
location: On the posterior midline, 1.5 cun directly above DU-16, in the depression superior to the external occipital protuberance
categories: Meeting point of Du Mai and Bladder channel
boardPearl: Located superior to the external occipital protuberance. Meeting of Du Mai and BL channel. 'Brain's Door' — the doorway to the brain. Treats head and brain disorders including epilepsy and mania.
memoryTrick: Brain's Door = the door to the brain on the back of the skull. Located above the occipital protuberance, 1.5 cun above DU-16 (Wind Palace).
cautions: Needle subcutaneously only.

name: Qiang Jian
location: On the posterior midline, 1.5 cun above DU-17, midway between DU-16 and DU-20
boardPearl: Located midway between DU-16 and DU-20. 'Unyielding Space' — the rigid area of the occiput. Less commonly tested individually.
memoryTrick: Unyielding Space = the unyielding (rigid) occiput. Midway between Wind Palace (DU-16) and Hundred Meetings (DU-20).
cautions: Needle subcutaneously only.

name: Hou Ding
location: On the posterior midline, 1.5 cun above DU-18, 1.5 cun posterior to DU-20
boardPearl: Located 1.5 cun behind the vertex (DU-20). 'Behind the Vertex' — posterior to the highest point of the skull. Less commonly tested.
memoryTrick: Behind the Vertex = 1.5 cun behind Bai Hui (DU-20). Think of it as DU-20's back neighbor.
cautions: Needle subcutaneously only.

name: Bai Hui
location: On the midline of the head, at the vertex, 5 cun directly above the midpoint of the anterior hairline, or at the midpoint of the line connecting the apices of both ears
categories: Meeting point of Du Mai with all six Yang channels (LI, SI, SJ, ST, BL, GB)
Meeting of Du Mai with Liver channel
boardPearl: EXTREMELY HIGH-YIELD. 'Hundred Meetings' = where a hundred (all) channels meet at the vertex. Meeting of Du Mai with the Liver channel and all Yang channels. The highest point on the body — Yang rises to its peak here. PRIMARY FUNCTION: raises Yang Qi — the #1 point for prolapse (rectal, uterine, organ). Also calms the spirit and benefits the brain. Think of it as the point that lifts everything up. Combined with REN-1 (lowest Yin) to treat prolapse. Moxa for raising Yang; needle for calming the spirit.
memoryTrick: Hundred Meetings = 100 channels meet at the crown. The highest point = raises Yang. Think: 'Everything that falls down (prolapse) needs Bai Hui to lift it up.' Vertex of the head, midpoint between the ears.
cautions: Contraindicated for needling in infants with an open fontanelle.
specialCategories: Meeting of Du with all Yang channels and LV

name: Qian Ding
location: On the midline of the head, 1.5 cun anterior to DU-20
boardPearl: Located 1.5 cun anterior to DU-20. 'Before the Vertex' — in front of the highest point. Less commonly tested individually.
memoryTrick: Before the Vertex = 1.5 cun in front of Bai Hui (DU-20). Think of it as DU-20's front neighbor.
cautions: Needle subcutaneously only.

name: Xin Hui
location: On the midline of the head, 3 cun anterior to DU-20, 2 cun posterior to the anterior hairline
boardPearl: Located at the area of the anterior fontanelle. 'Fontanelle Meeting' — where the skull bones meet at the fontanelle. Treats head disorders and infantile convulsions.
memoryTrick: Fontanelle Meeting = where the fontanelle meets (skull bones join). 3 cun in front of Bai Hui. Think baby's soft spot.
cautions: Contraindicated for needling in infants with an open fontanelle.

name: Shang Xing
location: On the midline of the head, 1 cun posterior to the anterior hairline
boardPearl: Located 1 cun behind the anterior hairline. 'Upper Star' — a star shining on the upper forehead. Important for nasal and eye disorders due to its location on the forehead. Often used for nasal congestion and frontal headaches.
memoryTrick: Upper Star = a star above the forehead, 1 cun behind the hairline. Think: 'The star lights up the nose and eyes below it.' Treats nasal and frontal disorders.

name: Shen Ting
location: On the midline of the head, 0.5 cun posterior to the anterior hairline
categories: Meeting point of Du Mai with Bladder and Stomach channels
boardPearl: Located 0.5 cun behind the anterior hairline. Meeting of Du, BL, and ST channels. 'Spirit Court' — the courtyard of the Shen (spirit). Important for calming the spirit and treating mental/emotional disorders — anxiety, insomnia, palpitations, mania. The spirit's court is at the front of the head where consciousness resides.
memoryTrick: Spirit Court = the court (courtyard) where the spirit (Shen) resides, 0.5 cun behind the hairline. Think: 'The spirit holds court at the front of the head — calming the mind and treating mania.'

name: Su Liao
location: On the tip of the nose, at the apex of the nose
depth: 0.2-0.3 cun obliquely superiorly, or prick to bleed
boardPearl: Located at the tip of the nose. 'Su Liao' (White Bone Hole) — the white refers to the Lung (Metal = white), and the nose is the opening of the Lung. Important for emergency revival (loss of consciousness) — stimulating the nasal tip can restore consciousness. Also treats nasal disorders and rosacea.
memoryTrick: White Bone Hole = white (Lung/Metal) at the nose tip. The nose is the Lung's orifice. Think: 'Pinch the nose tip to wake someone up — Su Liao for emergencies.' Also for rhinitis and rosacea.

name: Shui Gou
location: On the face, at the junction of the upper third and lower two-thirds of the philtrum (the groove between the nose and upper lip)
categories: Meeting point of Du Mai with LI and ST channels
boardPearl: EXTREMELY HIGH-YIELD. Also known as Ren Zhong ('Person's Center'). THE primary emergency revival point in Chinese medicine — used for all forms of loss of consciousness, fainting, shock, and collapse. Meeting of Du with LI and ST channels. Ghost point (Gui Gong — Ghost Palace). Located at the junction of the upper 1/3 and lower 2/3 of the philtrum. Also remarkably effective for acute lumbar sprain (empirical use). The philtrum is the 'water trough' between the nose (heaven/Du) and mouth (earth/Ren) — it is where Du and Ren Qi meet on the face.
memoryTrick: Water Trough = the philtrum is shaped like a water trough. Also called Ren Zhong (Center of the Person). Think: 'When someone faints, press DU-26 — it's the #1 emergency point in TCM.' Upper 1/3 to lower 2/3 of the philtrum. Ghost point for mania.

name: Dui Duan
location: On the face, at the junction of the philtrum and the upper lip, at the vermilion border of the upper lip, on the midline
boardPearl: Located at the vermilion border of the upper lip on the midline. 'Extremity of the Mouth' — the tip/edge of the mouth (upper lip). Treats mental disorders and mouth/nose conditions. The second-to-last point on Du Mai before it enters the mouth.
memoryTrick: Extremity of the Mouth = the extreme edge (vermilion border) of the upper lip. Almost at the end of the Du channel — one more point to go (DU-28 inside the mouth).

name: Yin Jiao
location: Inside the mouth, at the junction of the upper lip frenulum and the upper gum
depth: 0.1-0.2 cun obliquely superiorly, or prick to bleed
categories: Meeting point of Du Mai and Ren Mai
boardPearl: The terminal point of Du Mai. Meeting of Du Mai and Ren Mai — this is where the Yang (Du) meets the Yin (Ren), completing the Du-Ren circuit inside the upper lip. 'Gum Intersection' = where Du and Ren intersect at the gum. Together with REN-24 (below the lower lip), these points close the Ren-Du circuit. The tongue touching the palate in Qigong simulates this connection.
memoryTrick: Gum Intersection = Du and Ren intersect at the upper gum (inside the mouth). The final point of Du Mai completes the great Yin-Yang circuit. Don't confuse with REN-7 (also named Yin Jiao). Think: 'Du ends where Ren begins — inside the mouth at the gum.'
cautions: Prick to bleed is most common technique. Rarely needled deeply.

name: Si Shen Cong
location: A group of 4 points, at the vertex of the head, 1 cun anterior, posterior and lateral to DU-20 (Bai Hui)
depth: Transverse insertion 0.5-1 cun directed towards DU-20
boardPearl: Four points surrounding DU-20, each 1 cun away — commonly tested as a group for insomnia, epilepsy, and poor memory. Often combined with DU-20 for vertex headache.
memoryTrick: Four SPIRITS guard the CROWN (DU-20) like compass points — they keep the spirit calm for sleep and memory.

name: Dang Yang
location: On the head, directly above the pupil, 1 cun above the midpoint of the anterior hairline
boardPearl: Located directly above the pupil and 1 cun above the anterior hairline — often confused with BL-2 or GB-14 on exams. Remember it is an extra point above the hairline.
memoryTrick: DANG — right where your BANGS dangle, 1 cun into the hairline above the pupil.

name: Yin Tang
location: Midway between the medial ends of the two eyebrows
depth: Transverse insertion 0.3-0.5 cun inferiorly or pinch and needle 0.3-0.5 cun
boardPearl: One of the highest-yield extra points on board exams. Key functions: calms the spirit, benefits the nose, and activates the channel. Located at the 'third eye' — tested for insomnia, anxiety, frontal headache, and nasal disorders.
memoryTrick: YIN TANG = the hall (tang) of your IMPRESSION (third eye). The calm, quiet yin place between your brows where you focus and meditate — calms the spirit.
specialCategories: One of the most commonly used extra points

name: Yu Yao
location: On the forehead, directly above the pupil, at the midpoint of the eyebrow
depth: Transverse insertion 0.3-0.5 cun along the eyebrow
indications: Redness and swelling of the eye
boardPearl: Located at the midpoint of the eyebrow directly above the pupil — the key extra point for ptosis (drooping eyelid) and supraorbital pain. Distinguished from BL-2 (medial eyebrow) and SJ-23 (lateral eyebrow).
memoryTrick: The FISH WAIST is the middle of the fish (eyebrow) — the belly/waist of that little eyebrow fish swims right above the pupil.

name: Tai Yang
location: In the temporal region, in the depression approximately 1 cun posterior to the midpoint between the lateral end of the eyebrow and the outer canthus of the eye
depth: Perpendicular or oblique insertion 0.3-0.5 cun, or prick to bleed
boardPearl: The quintessential point for temporal headache and migraine. Frequently pricked to bleed for acute conditions. Located in the temple depression — boards love to test its location relative to the lateral eyebrow and outer canthus.
memoryTrick: TAI YANG (the SUN) sits at the TEMPLE — where you feel the throbbing sun-like pain of a migraine. Temples are where you press when you have a headache.
cautions: Avoid deep needling — temporal artery in the area. Prick to bleed with caution.

name: Er Jian
location: On the ear, at the apex of the auricle when the ear is folded towards the tragus
depth: Prick to bleed (most common method), or 0.1 cun perpendicular
boardPearl: Primary method is pricking to bleed — classic board question for acute eye inflammation (conjunctivitis) and clearing heat. The ear is folded forward to find the highest point.
memoryTrick: The EAR's pointy APEX (tip) when folded — like a mountain peak that you BLEED to let the heat escape, cooling red inflamed eyes.
cautions: Prick to bleed only — do not retain needles at this point.

name: Qiu Hou
location: At the junction of the lateral quarter and medial three-quarters of the infraorbital margin
depth: Slowly push the eyeball upward and insert the needle perpendicularly 0.5-1.5 cun along the orbital wall, without manipulating
boardPearl: One of the most dangerous extra points — needle goes behind the eyeball along the orbital floor. No manipulation allowed. Tested for serious/deep eye diseases like optic atrophy and retinitis pigmentosa.
memoryTrick: QIU (ball) HOU (behind) — literally BEHIND THE BALL (eyeball). Think: going deep behind the eye to treat the serious stuff.
cautions: Extremely dangerous point. No needle manipulation. Do not withdraw and reinsert. Requires advanced training. Press firmly after withdrawal to prevent hematoma.

name: Shang Ying Xiang
location: At the junction of the nasal bone and the lateral nasal cartilage, near the upper end of the nasolabial groove
depth: Perpendicular insertion 0.3-0.5 cun upward along the nasal bone
boardPearl: Located above LI-20 (Ying Xiang/Welcome Fragrance) — hence 'Upper Welcome Fragrance.' Both treat nasal disorders but this one is the extra point. Boards test the distinction between LI-20 and EX-HN-8.
memoryTrick: SHANG (upper) YING XIANG — it is literally the UPPER version of LI-20 (Welcome Fragrance). LI-20 welcomes fragrance at the nostril; this one is a step higher on the nose.

name: Nei Ying Xiang
location: On the mucous membrane of the nasal cavity, at the junction of the inferior turbinate bone and the lateral nasal wall
depth: Prick to bleed with a three-edged needle
boardPearl: Located INSIDE the nostril on the mucous membrane — pricked to bleed, not needled conventionally. Boards may test that this is the interior counterpart to LI-20 and EX-HN-8.
memoryTrick: NEI (inner) YING XIANG — the INSIDE-the-nose version of Welcome Fragrance. LI-20 is outside, EX-HN-8 is above, and EX-HN-9 is INSIDE.
cautions: Prick to bleed only on the nasal mucosa. Maintain sterile technique.

name: Ju Quan
location: On the tongue, at the midpoint of the dorsal surface when the tongue is rolled upward
boardPearl: Located on the midpoint of the dorsal surface of the tongue — an unusual location. Primarily pricked to bleed or treated with moxa for asthma, vomiting, and tongue ulcers.
memoryTrick: JU QUAN (Gathering Spring) — the SPRING gathers on the tongue's UPPER surface. Tongue rolled up to find the gathering point.
cautions: Prick to bleed only. Use caution with the sensitive tongue tissue.

name: Hai Quan
location: On the midline of the frenulum of the tongue, at the midpoint of the frenulum connecting the tongue to the floor of the mouth
boardPearl: Located on the midline frenulum under the tongue — a SEA SPRING of saliva. Primarily bled. Boards may test it alongside Jin Jin/Yu Ye (EX-HN-12) which are the sublingual veins flanking this point.
memoryTrick: HAI (sea) QUAN (spring) — the SEA SPRING sits where saliva pools under the tongue on the frenulum. The sea of fluid under your tongue.
cautions: Prick to bleed only.

name: Jin Jin / Yu Ye
location: On the sublingual veins on either side of the frenulum of the tongue. Jin Jin is on the left vein, Yu Ye is on the right vein.
depth: Prick the sublingual veins to bleed with a three-edged needle
boardPearl: Classic board question — two sublingual veins bled for aphasia, vomiting, and tongue stiffness. Jin Jin (Golden Liquid) is LEFT, Yu Ye (Jade Fluid) is RIGHT. Always prick to bleed, never retain needles.
memoryTrick: GOLDEN liquid (Jin Jin) on the LEFT, JADE fluid (Yu Ye) on the RIGHT — precious metals and gems under the tongue. Think of the two visible blue veins under your tongue — prick them to release speech (aphasia).
cautions: Prick to bleed only. Do not needle deeply. Ensure hemostasis after bleeding.

name: Yi Ming
location: On the neck, 1 cun posterior to SJ-17 (Yi Feng), which is behind the earlobe
boardPearl: Located 1 cun posterior to SJ-17 — boards love the spatial relationship. Treats both eye AND ear disorders (unique combination). Name 'Shielding Brightness' hints at its eye function.
memoryTrick: YI MING (Shielding BRIGHTNESS) is 1 cun BEHIND SJ-17 (Yi Feng/Shielding WIND). Two 'shielding' (Yi) points behind the ear — Feng for wind/ear, Ming for brightness/eyes.

name: Bi Tong
location: At the highest point of the nasolabial groove, at the junction of the nasal bone and nasal cartilage (near the upper end of LI-20)
depth: Upward oblique insertion 0.3-0.5 cun along the nasal bone
boardPearl: Name says it all: BI (nose) TONG (clear/unblock). Located near EX-HN-8 (Shang Ying Xiang) at the top of the nasolabial groove. Boards test the distinction between LI-20, EX-HN-8, and EX-HN-14 — all in the nasal area.
memoryTrick: BI TONG = NOSE CLEAR. It does exactly what its name says. When the nose is stuffed, Bi Tong clears it. Located at the top of the crease beside the nose.

name: Jing Bai Lao
location: On the neck, 2 cun directly above DU-14 (Da Zhui), and 1 cun lateral to the midline
boardPearl: Located 2 cun above DU-14 and 1 cun lateral — boards test exact location. Name references the 'hundred labors' of the neck. Treats both neck pain AND lung conditions (cough, asthma, whooping cough) plus scrofula.
memoryTrick: JING (neck) BAI LAO (hundred labors) — the NECK does a HUNDRED LABORS holding up the heavy head all day. 2 up from DU-14, 1 out to the side.

name: Ding Chuan
location: On the back, 0.5 cun lateral to the lower border of the spinous process of the 7th cervical vertebra (DU-14, Da Zhui)
boardPearl: Highest-yield back extra point on boards. DING CHUAN = STOP ASTHMA — its name IS its function. Located 0.5 cun lateral to DU-14. Frequently combined with BL-13 (Fei Shu) and Ren-17 for asthma protocols.
memoryTrick: DING (calm/stop) CHUAN (asthma/wheezing) — STOP the WHEEZE. Sits right next to DU-14 like a little asthma button, 0.5 cun to the side.

name: Jia Ji (Hua Tuo Jia Ji)
location: On the back and lumbar region, 17 pairs of points, 0.5 cun lateral to the lower border of each spinous process from the 1st thoracic vertebra (T1) to the 5th lumbar vertebra (L5)
depth: Perpendicular insertion 0.5-1 cun at thoracic level; 1-1.5 cun at lumbar level
indications: Spinal disorders corresponding to their segmental level
Upper Jia Ji points treat upper body/lung/heart disorders
Lower Jia Ji points treat lower body/kidney/intestine disorders
boardPearl: 34 points total (17 pairs), 0.5 cun lateral to each spinous process T1-L5. Boards test: Jia Ji are 0.5 cun lateral (vs. inner BL line at 1.5 cun). Named after the famous physician Hua Tuo. Each pair corresponds to the segmental level of the spine and related organs.
memoryTrick: HUA TUO's JIA JI — the famous surgeon Hua Tuo created these points LINING THE SPINE. 0.5 cun out from each vertebra, T1 to L5. Think: Hua Tuo lines the entire spine with 17 pairs of healing points.
cautions: Thoracic level — angle slightly medially to avoid pneumothorax risk.

name: Pi Gen
location: On the lower back, 3.5 cun lateral to the lower border of the spinous process of the 1st lumbar vertebra (L1)
boardPearl: Located 3.5 cun lateral to L1 — treats enlarged spleen and liver (hepatosplenomegaly) and abdominal masses. The name 'Spleen Root' directly indicates its primary function for spleen pathology.
memoryTrick: PI (spleen) GEN (root) — the ROOT of the SPLEEN. Go to the root of the problem when the spleen is enlarged. 3.5 cun lateral to L1, far out on the back.
cautions: Avoid deep needling on the right side over the kidney area.

name: Yao Yan
location: On the lower back, in the depression 3.5 cun lateral to the lower border of the spinous process of the 4th lumbar vertebra (L4)
boardPearl: The 'lumbar eyes' are the two visible depressions on the lower back at L4 level, 3.5 cun lateral. Boards test: these are the dimples visible on many people's lower backs. Treats lumbar pain and urogenital disorders.
memoryTrick: YAO (lumbar) YAN (eyes) — the two EYES (dimples) of the LOWER BACK that you can see on someone's back. The two depressions that 'look at you' from the lumbar region.

name: Yao Qi
location: On the sacrum, 2 cun directly above the tip of the coccyx, in the sacral hiatus
boardPearl: Located 2 cun above the coccyx tip in the sacral hiatus. Board favorite: an important point for EPILEPSY despite its low back location (qi ascends through the Du channel). Name 'Lumbar Oddity' reflects its unusual function.
memoryTrick: YAO (lumbar) QI (odd/strange) — the ODD/STRANGE lumbar point. It is ODD because a point near the tailbone treats EPILEPSY (a head condition). The Du mai connects them.

name: Shi Qi Zhui Xia
location: On the midline of the lower back, in the depression below the spinous process of the 5th lumbar vertebra (L5)
boardPearl: Located below L5 on the midline — the 17th vertebra counting from T1. Board classic for DYSMENORRHEA (painful periods). Often combined with SP-6 and Ren-3 for menstrual disorders. On the Du channel between DU-3 and DU-2.
memoryTrick: SHI QI (seventeen) ZHUI (vertebra) XIA (below) — BELOW the 17th VERTEBRA. Count: 12 thoracic + 5 lumbar = 17. Below L5, the magic spot for menstrual cramps.

name: Zhou Jian
location: On the posterior aspect of the elbow, at the tip of the olecranon process when the elbow is flexed
depth: Moxibustion is the primary method (3-7 cones); needling 0.3-0.5 cun if used
boardPearl: Primarily a MOXA point, not typically needled. Located at the tip of the olecranon (elbow point). Boards test: this point treats scrofula and carbuncles primarily through moxibustion, not acupuncture.
memoryTrick: ZHOU (elbow) JIAN (tip) — the TIP of the ELBOW. Sharp point of the elbow, and you mainly BURN moxa on it for lumps and abscesses.
cautions: Moxibustion is the primary treatment method at this point.

name: Er Bai
location: On the palmar aspect of the forearm, 4 cun proximal to the transverse wrist crease, on either side of the tendon of flexor carpi radialis (two points, one on each side of the tendon)
boardPearl: Two points flanking the flexor carpi radialis tendon, 4 cun above the wrist crease. Board favorite: a forearm point that treats HEMORRHOIDS and rectal prolapse. The mechanism is lifting qi — similar to DU-20 and BL-57.
memoryTrick: ER BAI (Two Whites) — TWO WHITE points on the pale forearm that treat your BEHIND (hemorrhoids). Two points, two cheeks — a memorable pairing.

name: Yao Tong Xue
location: On the dorsum of the hand, two points: one between the 2nd and 3rd metacarpal bones, and one between the 4th and 5th metacarpal bones, at the midpoint of the metacarpal bones just distal to the base
depth: Perpendicular insertion 0.5-0.8 cun directed towards the palm
boardPearl: Two points on the dorsum of the hand for ACUTE LUMBAR SPRAIN — a board classic. Patient makes a fist while points are needled, and actively moves the lumbar spine. Mechanism is distal point treatment. Extremely effective for acute low back pain.
memoryTrick: YAO TONG XUE = LUMBAR PAIN POINT — the name IS the indication. Two points on the back of the hand that magically treat the lower back. Needled while the patient moves their back.
specialCategories: Distal treatment for acute lumbar sprain

name: Wai Lao Gong (Luo Zhen)
location: On the dorsum of the hand, between the 2nd and 3rd metacarpal bones, 0.5 cun proximal to the metacarpophalangeal joint (opposite to PC-8 Lao Gong)
boardPearl: Also called LUO ZHEN (stiff neck/falling from pillow). Located on the dorsum of the hand opposite PC-8 (Lao Gong). THE point for acute stiff neck/torticollis — patient rotates neck while point is stimulated. Board classic distal point.
memoryTrick: LUO ZHEN means 'fell off the pillow' (woke up with a stiff neck). WAI LAO GONG = OUTER Lao Gong — it is on the back of the hand opposite PC-8. Wake up with a stiff neck? Needle the back of your hand.

name: Ba Xie
location: On the dorsum of both hands, at the webs between each pair of fingers, at the junction of the red and white skin, 8 points in total (4 on each hand)
depth: Oblique insertion 0.5-0.8 cun proximally, or prick to bleed
indications: Pain and numbness of the fingers
Redness and swelling of the dorsum of the hand
boardPearl: 8 points total (4 per hand) in the finger webs on the dorsum. Boards test: Ba Xie (Eight Evils) for hands vs. Ba Feng (Eight Winds, EX-LE-10) for feet — they are the hand/foot equivalents. Both treat pain, numbness, and can be bled.
memoryTrick: BA (eight) XIE (evils/pathogens) — EIGHT points that drive out the EVILS from between the fingers. Ba Xie = hands, Ba Feng = feet. XIE (evil) for upper, FENG (wind) for lower.

name: Si Feng
location: On the palmar surface of the index, middle, ring, and little fingers, at the midpoint of the proximal interphalangeal (PIP) joint transverse crease, 4 points on each hand (8 total)
depth: Prick with a three-edged needle or thick filiform needle to squeeze out yellowish viscous fluid
boardPearl: THE classic board point for childhood malnutrition (Gan syndrome/nutritional impairment). Located at the PIP joint creases of 4 fingers. Method: PRICK to squeeze out yellowish viscous fluid — not regular needling. The fluid's appearance indicates the severity of malnutrition.
memoryTrick: SI (four) FENG (seams/creases) — the FOUR SEAMS (creases) at the PIP joints. Prick the finger creases of malnourished CHILDREN to squeeze out the stagnation. Think of little kids' four fingers getting a quick prick.
cautions: Prick to bleed/express fluid only. Primarily used in pediatrics. Maintain strict sterile technique.

name: Shi Xuan
location: On the tips of all ten fingers, approximately 0.1 cun distal to each fingernail
depth: Prick to bleed (0.1-0.2 cun) with a three-edged needle
indications: Emergency revival from coma/loss of consciousness
boardPearl: All 10 fingertips BLED for EMERGENCY REVIVAL — the classic emergency extra point set. Boards test: Shi Xuan for loss of consciousness, high fever, and acute emergencies. Similar to Jing-Well points in emergency function but these are extra points at the very tips.
memoryTrick: SHI (ten) XUAN (dispersions) — TEN DISPERSIONS at TEN fingertips to disperse the emergency. Like the 12 Jing-Well points but simplified to 10 fingertips for emergencies. Prick all ten to wake someone up.
cautions: Emergency point — prick to bleed only.

name: Kuan Gu
location: On the thigh, at the midpoint of the upper border of the patella and the lateral end of the inguinal crease (at the midpoint of a line connecting the anterosuperior iliac spine and the lateral border of the patella)
indications: Weakness and atrophy of the lower limbs
boardPearl: Located at the midpoint between the ASIS and the lateral patella border — a midthigh point for hip and thigh disorders. Boards test location relative to the ASIS and patella.
memoryTrick: KUAN GU (hip bone) — the HIP BONE point. It sits at the midpoint of the thigh, connecting the hip bone (ASIS) area to the knee (patella). Halfway down the highway from hip to knee.

name: He Ding
location: On the knee, at the midpoint of the superior border (upper edge) of the patella
boardPearl: Located at the midpoint of the SUPERIOR patellar border — the 'summit' (top) of the kneecap. Boards test: He Ding is above the patella vs. Xi Yan (EX-LE-5) which is below. Key local knee point.
memoryTrick: HE (crane) DING (summit/top) — the CRANE'S SUMMIT. A crane stands on one leg with its knobbly knee sticking up — the top of that bony knee is He Ding. The summit (top) of the patella.

name: Nei Xi Yan
